title: Extend harness engineering model: compositional correctness gap
description: The LED magnetic letters architect discovered that all 4 gate layers pass geometry that is non-functional (cut-channel LED routing produces valid manifold that doesn't work). Dylan connected this to SendCutSend DFM failures: individual operations (cuts, bends, hardware) validate independently but composed assembly can interfere. This is the same structure as compliance vs coordination failure in the harness model — each agent correct, collective result degraded. Proposed extension: 'compositional correctness gap' as a named failure mode class. Every gate stack eventually needs a gate operating one abstraction level above the others. Update harness-engineering model.md §8 (compliance vs coordination) to generalize: these are instances of the same pattern at different scales (operation→assembly, agent→system). Add LED gate stack and sheet metal DFM as cross-domain evidence.

title: orch init should register project with daemon for cross-project polling
description: New projects initialized with orch init are invisible to the daemon. The daemon has --group support via groups.yaml, but: (1) no groups.yaml exists by default, (2) orch init doesn't create or update one, (3) there's no orch project register or similar command. Result: orchestrators in new projects assume daemon can't poll cross-project and spawn directly every time. First observed in led-magnetic-letters — triage:ready issues sat with 'No spawnable issues in queue' in daemon log. Fix options: orch init auto-registers in ~/.orch/groups.yaml, or new orch project add command, or daemon polls all known .beads/ dirs without groups.

title: Redesign orch review to produce batch synthesis — collect completed agent artifacts, synthesize across findings, surface what changed in understanding before completing
description:
Dylan's primary comprehension gap: review workflow returns bare numbered lists instead of synthesis. Pattern: agents finish → Dylan asks 'what's ready to review' → orchestrator returns checklist → Dylan has to ask separately for synthesis.

Root cause: orch review optimizes for completion throughput (close agents fast) not comprehension (what do results mean together). Three-Layer Reconnection fires per-agent, not across the batch. explain_back/verified gates are captured by AI orchestrator (rubber-stamped).

Proposed fix: orch review becomes the synthesis point. Command should:
1. Collect all completed agent SYNTHESIS.md / investigation artifacts
2. Read and cross-reference findings
3. Produce a single batch synthesis — what we now know that we didn't before, connected to active threads
4. Then complete the agents

This replaces explain_back and verified gates as the comprehension enforcement mechanism. The synthesis IS the review, not a side effect of it.

Context: kb-824c2f (gate capture decision), kb-31a6b4 (review synthesis constraint). Thread: exploration mode conversation surfaced push-vs-pull comprehension gap.

title: Phase 2b: Allocation Profile — skill-aware slot scoring in daemon
Detect stalled agents: running but no token progress
description:
Detect stalled agents using OpenCode session infrastructure instead of token count polling.

CONTEXT: Agents can get stuck in "running" state. Current detection requires manual orch status comparison. But OpenCode already has the signals we need.

BETTER APPROACH (from opencode-fork and agent-lifecycle models):

1. GET /session/status already exists — returns idle/busy/retry per session
2. SSE event stream already flows to daemon — session.status, message.part.updated events
3. Session metadata API (implemented Feb 14) can store beads_id at spawn time

STALL DETECTION:
- Session status = "busy" (model claims to be working)
- No SSE events (message.part.updated, part.updated) for >3 minutes
- This means "busy but silent" = stalled
- Distinct from "idle" (model finished) and "busy with output" (model working)

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. In daemon SSE listener, track last-event-time per session
2. If session is busy and last-event-time > 3 minutes ago, flag as STALLED
3. Surface in dashboard as distinct state (not idle, not busy — stalled)
4. Surface in orch status: "⚠️ STALLED (busy but no output for 3m)"
5. Store beads_id in session metadata at spawn time for direct correlation

DO NOT: Poll token counts via orch status comparison (hacky, unreliable)
USE: OpenCode native signals (session.status + SSE event recency)

REFERENCES:
- .kb/models/opencode-fork.md — GET /session/status, session metadata API, SSE events
- .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model.md — Failure Mode 3: Agent Went Idle But Not Complete
- .kb/models/opencode-session-lifecycle.md — SSE event structure, completion detection

VERIFICATION:
- Agent that stalls (no output for 3m while busy) gets flagged in dashboard
- orch status shows STALLED state
- No false positives on agents doing long tool calls (tool calls still emit SSE events)
- Stall detection uses OpenCode SSE events, not token count polling

title: Implement: Designed orchestrator-as-scoping-agent architecture that separates judgment (orchestrator enriches issues) from execution (daemon spawns agents). Key mechanisms: remove orch spawn from orchestrator tool space, comprehension queue with daemon throttle, two-phase completion, bypass measurement. 7 forks analyzed, 5 navigated, 2 blocking questions surfaced. (from architect orch-go-hlklh)
description:
4-phase implementation of orchestrator-as-scoping-agent (from architect orch-go-hlklh):

Phase 1: Enrichment Protocol (behavioral, no code)
- Update orchestrator SKILL.md: remove orch spawn from tool space
- Add enrichment guidance: when to add skill:*, area:* labels
- Add description quality guidance

Phase 2: Comprehension Queue Infrastructure
- Add comprehension:pending label support
- Daemon auto-complete adds label after closing
- Daemon throttle: check pending count before spawn cycle
- Add orch comprehension command: list pending, mark reviewed
- Add UserPromptSubmit hook injecting queue count every turn (~37ms, negligible)

Phase 3: Two-Phase Completion
- orch complete removes comprehension:pending
- Three-Layer Reconnection as structured prompt
- Track comprehension time per issue

Phase 4: Bypass Measurement
- spawn.bypass event when orch spawn used directly by Dylan
- Bypass rate in orch stats
- Alert if bypass exceeds 20%

Design inputs: overnight exception (daemon runs freely, queue grows, orchestrator catches up at session start). Blocking questions answered: targeted skill labeling (B), inline human-only.

Success criteria: skill:* label usage >30%, comprehension queue bounded (<2x threshold), bypass rate <20%, zero comprehension:pending >48h old.

title: Implement: Designed 4-level compliance dial (Strict/Standard/Relaxed/Autonomous) with per-(skill, model) resolution. Safety gates (dedup, circuit breakers) are always-on; compliance gates (extraction, architect escalation, verification threshold, verify levels) are tunable. The verify package's existing V0-V3 system is the foundation — compliance just caps it. Migration in 3 phases, backward-compatible (default = Strict = current behavior). (from architect orch-go-nlrzw)
description:
Auto-created from architect review orch-go-nlrzw.

## Architect Summary
Designed 4-level compliance dial (Strict/Standard/Relaxed/Autonomous) with per-(skill, model) resolution. Safety gates (dedup, circuit breakers) are always-on; compliance gates (extraction, architect escalation, verification threshold, verify levels) are tunable. The verify package's existing V0-V3 system is the foundation — compliance just caps it. Migration in 3 phases, backward-compatible (default = Strict = current behavior).

## Architect Next Section
**Recommendation:** spawn-follow-up

### Implementation Issues

Three implementation phases, each as a separate issue:

**Phase 1: ComplianceConfig type + resolution**
- Add ComplianceLevel type, ComplianceConfig struct, Resolve() method to pkg/daemonconfig
- Parse from ~/.orch/config.yaml
- Wire into Daemon struct
- Add `orch compliance status` command
- Default = Strict = no behavioral change

**Phase 2: Gate compliance-awareness**
- Make architect escalation, VerificationTracker, InvariantChecker compliance-aware
- Pass compliance level through spawn context to verify package
- Cap verify levels and review tiers based on compliance level

**Phase 3: Measurement feedback loop**
- Add compliance level to events
- Per-(skill, model) metric collection
- `orch compliance suggest` command

title: Daemon duplicate spawn loop - issue stays triage:ready after first agent spawned
description: Three agents spawned for the same ci-implement-role work because the issue remained triage:ready after the first spawn. The daemon's dedup TTL cache expired between spawns, allowing re-spawning of already-in-progress work. Root cause: daemon should mark issue in_progress immediately on spawn to prevent re-spawn. Currently relies on TTL cache which is fragile. The 3 duplicate agents all detected the duplication themselves and reported it, but the damage (wasted capacity) was already done.

title: Dashboard: split-and-grow node transformation animation
description:
When an agent completes and produces a knowledge artifact, animate the transformation from issue node to artifact node using split-and-grow.

DESIGN DOC: docs/designs/2026-02-15-collaborative-tree-building.md (Phase 3)

SCOPE:
1. Detect when an issue completion produces a .kb/ artifact (investigation, decision, model)
2. Animate: issue ● node fades/shrinks to small gray node
3. Animate: new ◉/★/◆ artifact node grows from it with branch line connecting them
4. Smooth CSS transitions for state changes (triage → in-progress → complete)
5. Pulsing stops and node solidifies on completion, then triggers split-and-grow

DEPENDS ON: orch-go-krgg (dashboard tree component)

VERIFICATION:
- When agent completes and produces investigation, split-and-grow animation plays
- Old issue node remains visible (small, gray) with connecting edge to new artifact
- Animation is smooth, not jarring
- Works for all artifact types (investigation, decision, model)

title: Experiment: Agent scaling — does structural placement degrade at N>2 with limited insertion points?
description:
## Context

Structural placement (Claim 2) works at N=2 with abundant insertion points — the file has many viable anchor functions. But the model has open questions about scaling: "Does placement work when the number of agents exceeds the number of natural insertion points?" (line 285) and "What is the minimum number of natural insertion points needed per agent?" (line 295).

## Task

Design and run a scaling experiment with 4-6 agents sharing a file with limited insertion regions.

**Design:** Same repo structure, but:
- 4 agents with additive tasks, only 3 viable insertion regions (forcing at least 2 agents to share a region)
- 6 agents with additive tasks, only 3-4 viable insertion regions

**Key questions:**
1. Does placement degrade gracefully (partial conflicts) or cliff-edge (all-or-nothing)?
2. What is the minimum ratio of insertion-points-to-agents for reliable coordination?
3. Does automated attractor discovery (which worked at N=2) still generate effective constraints at N=4+?

**Expected outcomes:**
- Graceful degradation → placement is robust, just needs more insertion points per agent
- Cliff-edge failure → there's a critical threshold, which is important for production daemon design
- Automated discovery fails at scale → constraint generation needs sophistication beyond collision parsing

**Constraints:**
- Use same model (claude-haiku-4-5)
- Design tasks that are comparable in complexity to existing simple/complex pairs
- Must actually create a file with limited natural insertion points (don't artificially constrain — the file structure should organically limit options)
- N=5 per condition minimum for statistical signal
- Reference: experiments/coordination-demo/redesign/ for harness, probes/automated-attractor-discovery for N=2 baseline

## What's known
- N=2 placement: 29/29 SUCCESS (20 main + 9 decay)
- Automated discovery: 7/7 SUCCESS from 2 observed collisions
- Current tasks have many viable insertion points (file has ~15 functions)
- No data exists for N>2

N-way merge 0/25 (total failure), pairwise merges mostly work. New finding: import blocks are a conflict source placement can't address. Probe merged into model.

title: Redesign coaching metrics + integrate into orch stats + auto-surface at session start
description:
Three-part feature:

## 1. Metric Redesign

ORCHESTRATOR - Strip noise, keep signal:
- KEEP: frame_collapse (delegation rule violation), circular_pattern (contradicting prior knowledge)
- KEEP but tune: behavioral_variation (useful for spawn retry loops)
- KILL: action_ratio (378 fires, wrong signal for orchestrators), analysis_paralysis (352 fires, consecutive bash is normal), compensation_pattern (79 fires, too loose at 30% overlap), context_ratio (unclear value), dylan_signal_prefix (never fired), premise_skipping (never fired)
- ADD: spawn_without_context (no kb context in recent tool window before orch spawn), completion_backlog (agents at Phase:Complete >10 min without orch complete), direct_implementation (Edit/Write on files outside .kb/ .orch/ CLAUDE.md AGENTS.md)

Result: 5 orchestrator metrics (2 kept, 3 new) down from 8 noisy ones.

WORKER - Keep all 4 as-is, let them accumulate real data:
- tool_failure_rate (3+ consecutive failures)
- context_usage (estimated tokens, alert at 80%)
- time_in_phase (minutes since phase change, alert at 15+)
- commit_gap (time since last commit, alert at 30+ min)

## 2. Stats Integration

Add coaching summary section to orch stats output:
```
🧠 BEHAVIORAL HEALTH (coaching plugin, last N days)
  Orchestrator:
    Frame collapse:       3 events (2 sessions)
    Circular patterns:    1 event
    Completion backlog:   2 alerts
    Direct implementation: 0 events
  Workers:
    Context exhaustion:   2 alerts (>80%)
    Tool failure runs:    1 alert
    Stuck in phase:       0 alerts
    Commit gaps:          4 alerts
```

Read from ~/.orch/coaching-metrics.jsonl, aggregate by metric_type for the stats time window.

## 3. Session Start Auto-Surface

Run orch stats --days 1 summary automatically when orchestrator session starts. Could be:
- Plugin hook on first tool call in new session
- session.prompt.before hook if available
- Injection via coaching plugin detecting new orchestrator session

Goal: orchestrator sees system health picture without remembering to run the command.

## Evidence
- coaching-metrics.jsonl has 1000+ entries, dominated by action_ratio (378) and analysis_paralysis (352) noise
- Worker metrics just started working (Feb 14 fix) - context_usage verified
- orch stats already reads events.jsonl, adding coaching-metrics.jsonl pass is natural
- Current session start is skill doc guideline only, not automated

## Key files
- plugins/coaching.ts (metric detection, 1831 lines)
- cmd/orch/stats.go (stats aggregation)
- .opencode/plugin/coaching.ts (deployed copy, must stay in sync)

title: Improve daemon skill inference with description-aware heuristics and accuracy tracking
description: Two gaps in daemon skill inference (pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go): (1) DESCRIPTION-AWARE HEURISTICS: Current inference only uses issue type + skill:* labels + title prefix. It never reads the description. Add keyword/pattern matching on description text to improve inference. Signal words: 'audit/analyze/correlate/investigate/understand/how does' → investigation, 'fix/broken/error/crash/fails' → systematic-debugging (when cause is described) or architect (when vague), 'compare/evaluate/research/best practice' → research. This should be a fallback AFTER label and title checks but BEFORE type-based inference. Keep it simple — keyword matching, not NLP. (2) INFERENCE ACCURACY TRACKING: Add an event type 'spawn.skill_inferred' to events.jsonl that logs {issue_id, inferred_skill, issue_type, title, had_skill_label, had_title_match, used_description_heuristic}. This enables post-hoc analysis of inference quality. Also fix: InferSkillFromTitle still references kb-reflect which is being removed (orch-go-bt7). Key files: pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go (114 lines), pkg/daemon/skill_inference_test.go, pkg/daemon/daemon.go (where inference is called). Verify: go test ./pkg/daemon/ passes, go build ./... passes. PROJECT_DIR: /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go

- ~/.claude/skills/ (checksum 61797c7c8a98, last modified Feb 14 18:43)  
- ~/.opencode/skill/ (checksum 779efe80fbde, last modified Feb 13 14:29)

Agent orch-go-6th updated the source on Feb 14 23:24 but never ran skillc deploy. The orchestrator that loaded the stale skill had zero instructions about verifiability-first, so it fell back to the old completion flow (orchestrator as judge, batch-completing light-tier) under velocity pressure.

FIX: Run skillc deploy to push source SKILL.md to both deployment targets. Verify checksums match after deploy.

EVIDENCE: grep for 'explain-back' in source returns 11 matches, in deployed returns 0.

title: Enrich spawn context with orch tree --cluster for area awareness
description:
Before spawning into an area, the orchestrator should see the full knowledge landscape for that area. This improves skill selection, prompt quality, and prevents duplicate work.

CONTEXT: Currently kb context provides keyword search. orch tree --cluster provides structural awareness — what investigations exist, what decisions were made, what is untested. Together they give the orchestrator full area context before spawning.

SCOPE:
1. When orchestrator runs orch spawn, auto-detect the area from issue labels or task description
2. If area maps to a known cluster, inject orch tree --cluster <area> --depth 3 output into the spawn prompt or show it to orchestrator before spawn confirmation
3. Add orch tree --cluster <area> --format summary flag that produces a 3-5 line area briefing:
   - N investigations, M decisions, K models, J open issues
   - Health smells if any
   - Most recent artifact date
4. Consider adding this to SPAWN_CONTEXT.md template so agents also see where their work fits

DEPENDS ON: health smells and relationship parsing for full value, but basic cluster summary works with current tree.

VERIFICATION:
- orch tree --cluster X --format summary produces concise area briefing
- Spawning into an area with a known cluster surfaces the cluster context
- Agents see where their work fits in the knowledge landscape
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: Fix failing spawn exploration judge flag test
skillc test: multi-turn scenario support
description: Extend skillc test beyond --print (single-shot) to support 2-3 turn conversations with simulated tool responses. This is the critical ecological validity fix — the skill's real value is in recovery and tool selection, which require multi-turn interaction. Minimum: prompt → response → correction/follow-up → response. Simulated tool availability so the model's behavior reflects real affordances. This bridges the gap between --print baseline (bare-parity on 5/7 scenarios) and the grammar recovery validation (passing in real interactive sessions). Evidence: investigation 2026-03-01 behavioral baseline, grammar recovery validation 2026-03-01.

title: [orch-go] systematic-debugging: Build is broken: cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go:536:1 ha...
Reorganize orchestrator skill around 'keep Dylan oriented' as primary frame
description:
The orchestrator skill (~1,251 lines) is currently organized around what the orchestrator does (delegate, triage, synthesize). But Dylan's experience of working with an orchestrator should feel like being narrated through a journey, not presented with checkboxes. The completion review section describes a mechanical two-gate process, but Dylan reports the ceremony feels disconnected — he lacks the frame to understand why completed work matters by the time it arrives.

Core insight from conversation: the comprehension problem isn't at completion time, it's at spawn time. The 'why Dylan cares' frame decays between spawn and completion, and nothing preserves it. The explain-back gate assumes Dylan has context he's already lost.

Proposed reframe: organize the entire skill around 'is Dylan oriented?' at each moment:
- At spawn: establish the frame (what are we doing and why)
- During work: maintain narrative continuity (things are progressing)  
- At completion: reconnect to original motivation, resolve, place contextually (1 of 3 blockers for X)
- At session boundaries: 'previously on...' orientation

The mechanical constraints (delegation rule, verification gates, daemon management) don't disappear but become implementation detail in service of orientation, not the organizing frame.

Key constraint: the skill is auto-generated from templates at ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/meta/orchestrator/.skillc/ — architect must propose structure changes to template sources, not SKILL.md directly.

Risk: current skill is battle-tested, every rule from a real failure. New frame must be at least as protective while being more coherent.

title: Implement orchestrator skill orientation redesign - template rewrite
description:
Implement the accepted design from orch-go-1003 (architect investigation at .kb/investigations/2026-02-16-design-orchestrator-skill-orientation-redesign.md).

Phase 1: Rewrite the orchestrator skill template at ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/meta/orchestrator/.skillc/

Key changes:
1. Reorganize top-level sections from action-based (COMPREHEND/TRIAGE/SYNTHESIZE) to four-moment structure (At Spawn Time / During Work / At Completion / At Session Boundaries)
2. Update identity from 'Strategic Comprehender' to 'Strategic Comprehender who keeps Dylan oriented' with ORIENT → DELEGATE → RECONNECT operating model
3. Replace completion review explain-back with three-layer reconnection model (frame reconnection, resolution, contextual placement) within Gate 1
4. Add frame capture checklist to spawn-time section
5. Consolidate hard constraints into dedicated section
6. Move session start/end protocols from buried Meta-Orchestrator Interface to top-level session boundaries section

Constraint mapping table in design investigation shows all load-bearing patterns survive.
~55% content stays in place, ~30% moves, ~10% new, ~5% removed (redundant).

Design investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-16-design-orchestrator-skill-orientation-redesign.md
Decision on identity: 'Strategic Comprehender who keeps Dylan oriented' (hybrid)
Decision on frame friction: medium (required field, defaults to task description)

title: Add ORIENTATION_FRAME to SPAWN_CONTEXT.md template and orch spawn
description:
Phase 2 of the orientation redesign (depends on orch-go-1004 skill rewrite).

Add ORIENTATION_FRAME section to SPAWN_CONTEXT.md template in pkg/spawn/context.go. Medium friction: required field that defaults to task description if orchestrator doesn't provide explicit frame.

The frame captures 'why Dylan cares' in his terms at spawn time, travels through the workspace/beads, and gets read back at completion time for the three-layer reconnection model.

Also add bd comment with FRAME: annotation at spawn time so orchestrator can read it back during orch complete.

title: bd sync hangs — second occurrence in 24 hours
description: bd sync is hanging repeatedly. First occurrence: earlier today during agent completion flow. Second occurrence: just now, orch status also timed out (likely calls beads internally). Earlier we saw bd sync fail with 'cannot pull with rebase: You have unstaged changes' — but that should be an error, not a hang. Two possibilities: (1) git lock contention from multiple agents committing simultaneously, (2) actual deadlock in bd sync logic. The beads repo had a recent fix for bd sync deadlock (commit in beads repo). Need to verify we're running the patched version and investigate whether the hang is git-level or beads-level.

title: Model strategy: evaluate Claude model lineup for orchestration workflow
description: Sonnet 4.6 launched (Feb 17 2026) — approaches Opus-level intelligence at Sonnet pricing. Users preferred it to Opus 4.5 59% of the time. Less overengineering, better instruction following, 1M context window. Anthropic says 'Opus 4.6 remains the strongest for deepest reasoning.' We currently default to sonnet for headless spawns and use --opus for complex skills (debugging, investigation, architect, audit, research). Questions to answer: (1) Should Sonnet 4.6 replace Opus for any currently-Opus skills? (2) Is Opus 4.6 still worth the Max subscription cost for agent work? (3) What's the right model for each skill type given current pricing and capability? (4) Should we add Sonnet 4.6 as an explicit model option in orch spawn? (5) How does the 1M context window change our context management strategy? Reference: https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-sonnet-4-6 Current model selection: skill/meta/orchestrator SKILL.md Model Selection section.

title: Fix dashboard blind to tmux agents: reorder scans in serve_agents.go
description: Root cause: completed workspace scan runs before tmux scan in serve_agents.go. Fix: (1) move tmux scan before workspace scan, (2) add beads_id to workspace duplicate check. See probe: .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model/probes/2026-02-17-dashboard-blind-to-tmux-agents.md

title: default_model: opus silently forces Claude CLI backend — model-backend conflation bug
description: Fixed: removed model-based backend auto-selection from extraction.go. Model choice and backend choice are independent. Commit: 80744166.

title: [orch-go] investigation: Trace the orchestrator skill injection path for...
Fix orchestrator-session.ts plugin to not cache skill content at init time - causes stale versions after skillc deploy
Fix wrong path in load-orchestration-context.py line 27 - missing meta/ prefix in skill path
Clean up 5 stale orchestrator skill copies across ~/.opencode/skill/ and ~/.claude/skills/src/
description: 7 copies of orchestrator skill exist. 5 are stale from old deployment paths that skillc deploy never cleans.

title: [orch-go] feature-impl: Fix skillc deploy to also target ~/.config/open...
Add default_model config key and GPT model aliases to enable configurable default model for worker spawns
Probe: How does the OpenAI/GPT plugin work in OpenCode? What models are available via OAuth, what format does it expect, and will orch's openai/gpt-4o model string work end-to-end?
Add Codex model aliases to orch and update default_model recommendations for GPT Pro OAuth path
Explicit --model and --backend flags should never be overridden by infrastructure escape hatch
description: When spawning with explicit --model codex --backend opencode, the infrastructure escape hatch silently overrides to --backend claude --tmux. This makes model evaluation impossible. Fix: explicit flags should always win over auto-detection. The escape hatch should only apply when no --model or --backend flag is given.

title: Tier inference assigns light to medium-scope work — new packages with tests get classified as light
description: The tier auto-assignment is too aggressive at classifying spawns as light. Creating a new package with algorithm implementation and comprehensive tests is clearly full-tier work. Fix: tier inference should consider scope indicators like 'create package', 'new module', test requirements, and SESSION SCOPE from the spawn prompt (which was explicitly set to Medium).

title: Fix dashboard blind to tmux agents: reorder scans in serve_agents.go
description:
Three compounding bugs make tmux agents invisible in dashboard:

1. Scan ordering (Critical): serve_agents.go workspace scan (line 598) runs before tmux scan (line 551). Workspace scan claims active tmux agents as 'completed'. Fix: move tmux scan before workspace scan.

2. Beads enrichment skip (Critical): serve_agents.go lines 700-703 skip beads phase comments for workspace-scanned entries, so phase is always null. Fix: include beads enrichment for entries that have a beads_id.

3. active_agent not populated (Important): serve_beads.go GraphNode struct lacks active_agent field. Frontend expects it. Fix: join agent data by beads_id in handleBeadsGraph().

4. WIP store stub (Nice-to-have): wip.ts setRunningAgents() is a no-op, making runningAgentDetailsByIssueId always empty for ALL agents.

Root cause documented in: .kb/models/agent-lifecycle-state-model/probes/2026-02-17-dashboard-blind-to-tmux-agents.md

title: Dashboard work-graph usability improvements: visibility, sorting, and clarity
description:
The work-graph Issues view is structurally working (dependency trees, Ready to Complete, independent issues) but has several usability gaps that make it hard to orient at a glance:

**1. In-progress issues don't visually stand out**
The play icon + green subline is too subtle. P1 in-progress bugs should pop — left border accent, background highlight, or similar treatment to the Ready to Complete section.

**2. P1s buried in INDEPENDENT ISSUES**
Two P1 bugs are mixed in with 25 P2s. Priority should sort within sections — P1s at top, not mixed by creation order.

**3. 'No active agent linked' is noisy and negative**
Shown on every in-progress issue (partly due to orch-go-1022 tmux bug). Even when fixed, the subline should show agent phase + runtime when linked, and just 'unassigned' or nothing when not — not an alarming message.

**4. Ready to Complete shows stale/already-closed entries**
980.1 (already closed) and 990 (failed Codex attempt with stale Phase: Complete) still appear. Section should only show issues that are actually open + Phase: Complete.

**5. 27 independent issues is overwhelming**
Most are backlog. Default should show active work (in-progress + recently created) and collapse/paginate the rest. Or separate 'Active' vs 'Backlog' sections.

**6. Completed agents section competes with Ready to Complete**
The completed agents banner (token counts, timing) is useful but visually competes with the more actionable Ready to Complete section. Consider collapsing by default or moving to Completed tab.

**7. Dependency chain header uses issue title as section name**
Chain header shows the title of issue 980.4 which reads oddly as a group name. Should use the root issue title, a computed chain name, or just the root issue ID.

title: Work-graph: Ready to Complete section doesn't appear in real-time
Design and implement orch rework command for sending agents back with specific completion review feedback
description: Completion review regularly finds gaps in agent work (e.g. xu8l shipped without removing flag definitions, sg4k sort has no effect). Currently the workaround is spawning a fresh agent with REWORK prefix in the prompt. A dedicated orch rework <beads-id> 'feedback' command would: (1) reopen the issue, (2) inject the rework feedback as context alongside the original SYNTHESIS.md, (3) spawn a new agent against the same issue with both the original task and the rework feedback, (4) track it as a rework (not a fresh attempt) for completion metrics. This is a natural extension of the completion workflow.

title: Implement orch rework command per design
DetermineSpawnBackend ignores user config (~/.orch/config.yaml) backend setting — only checks project config
description: DetermineSpawnBackend (extraction.go:612) has a config resolution bug. When --model is explicit but --backend is not, it checks projCfg.SpawnMode (project config) but never checks userconfig.Load().Backend (user config at ~/.orch/config.yaml). The user has backend: opencode in their global config, but projects without their own .orch/config.yaml fall through to the hardcoded default of claude. Fix: add user config fallback in the resolution chain: explicit --backend > project config > user config > hardcoded default.

title: orch work / daemon spawn ignores default_model from user config — always spawns on sonnet/claude
description: runWork() in spawn_cmd.go:284 calls runSpawnWithSkillInternal without setting spawnModel. The user config has default_model: codex but orch work never reads it. This means every daemon-spawned agent ignores the user's default model preference, burning Max subscription tokens on claude when they should be running on codex via opencode. Fix: runWork should load userconfig, read default_model, and set spawnModel before calling runSpawnWithSkillInternal. This is the daemon path — it affects ALL auto-spawned work.

title: Test issue for verifying daemon model selection
OpenAI/Codex auth is project-scoped in OpenCode — headless agents fail intermittently, price-watch can't use codex at all
description: Two related symptoms: (1) Three headless codex agents spawned at 10:12 AM dead (0 tokens), but one spawned at 10:54 works fine — suggesting transient or project-context-dependent initialization. (2) Price-watch project can't spawn codex agents despite valid auth.json — PW has a .env file that orch-go doesn't, and OpenCode may resolve auth differently per project. Root hypothesis: Codex plugin auth initialization is project-scoped, and something about per-project context (env files, config, provider state) affects whether OpenAI OAuth tokens are loaded.

title: State.create caches rejected Promises with no retry
description: Fix State.create to clear cache entry on Promise rejection or add retry logic. This is likely root cause of intermittent headless Codex agent failures (0 tokens, dead at spawn).

title: Audit fix: commits from entropy-spiral-feb2026 — of the ~161 fix commits, how many address bugs still present on current master? Categorize each as: (1) still-relevant (bug exists on master), (2) irrelevant (code diverged or feature doesn't exist), (3) already-fixed (same fix exists on master). FRAME: Dylan is hitting bugs that were already fixed on the spiral branch but lost in the rollback. We need to know the scale of the problem before deciding recovery strategy.
Add /api prefix mount to OpenCode server for external tool compatibility
description: The OpenCode server SPA catch-all is swallowing all /api/* routes, returning frontend HTML instead of JSON responses. This is the root cause of a cascade of observability failures: orch status shows phantom agents with wrong model/skill/runtime, orch review can't detect completions, dashboard Swarm Map shows 0 active agents, SSE stream receives no session events, work-graph shows unassigned instead of agent info. Spawn pipeline itself works (sessions are created and execute successfully), but nothing can observe them afterward. Discovered during price-watch codex spawn testing on 2026-02-18.

title: Tmux spawn times out — timeout waiting for OpenCode TUI to be ready after 15s
description: The --tmux flag triggers a TUI readiness check with a 15s timeout that fails. Headless spawns work fine. Discovered during price-watch codex spawn testing. The tmux spawn path uses opencode attach mode which requires the TUI to initialize — the readiness probe may be checking the wrong signal or the TUI startup is slower than 15s for cross-project directories.

title: Clean up stale phantom agents and session metrics — 55 phantoms, 689h session time
description: orch status shows 55 phantom agents and session metrics from a 689h-old session with goal 'OpenAI+OpenCode integration'. This is accumulated stale state that adds noise to status output and dashboard. Likely related to orch-go-1033 (API returning HTML means sessions can never be verified as alive/dead, so they accumulate as phantoms). After fixing orch-go-1033, run orch clean --stale to prune. The session metrics need a reset mechanism or should auto-expire.

title: Staleness audit of orchestrator skill — model selection references --opus/--sonnet which no longer exist, likely other stale references too
Fix 13 stale CLI references in orchestrator skill source — 7 harmful, 4 misleading, 2 cosmetic
description: Apply fixes identified in probe: .kb/models/orchestrator-session-lifecycle/probes/2026-02-18-orchestrator-skill-cli-staleness-audit.md

title: 96 phantom agents and stale session metrics — root cause unknown
description: orch status shows 96 phantom agents and session metrics from a 689h-old session. Originally attributed to OpenCode API routing (orch-go-1033) but that was disproven — orch-go's client uses /session not /api/session and those endpoints work fine. Server restart reduced phantoms from showing but didn't eliminate them. Possible causes: 1) Sessions accumulate in OpenCode's session store and are never cleaned up. 2) orch's registry tracks sessions that no longer exist on the server. 3) The /session/status endpoint returns {} (empty) which may cause orch to classify all sessions as phantom. 4) 689h session metrics suggest the session tracking state file is never reset. Need to understand the full lifecycle: how does a session go from spawned → active → complete → cleaned up, and where is that chain breaking?

title: [orch-go] investigation: tmux readiness probe check
tmux spawn fails inside overmind due to socket mismatch
description: When running orch spawn inside overmind tmux, SessionExists targets main socket while CreateWindow/SendKeys target current socket, so workers-* session appears to exist but window creation fails in overmind.

title: Fix orch status phantom logic — align with state.LivenessResult.IsPhantom
description: orch status in cmd/orch/status_cmd.go has its own phantom classification that marks agents as phantom without checking beads open/closed status. The canonical phantom logic in pkg/state/reconcile.go (LivenessResult.IsPhantom) correctly requires beads issue to be open. Fix: replace the status_cmd phantom logic with the reconcile.go implementation so completed agents are filtered out. This is the root cause of the inflated phantom count.

title: Add reconciliation pass to detect stale open beads issues with no live session
description: After fixing phantom classification (orch-go-1039), add a reconciliation command or daemon pass that detects open beads issues with no live session or tmux window. Surface these as candidates for orch abandon or orch complete --force. This prevents beads issues from staying open indefinitely when agents die without completing.

title: Auto-expire orchestrator session metrics — 689h unbounded timer
description: ~/.orch/session.json tracks orchestrator session start time but never expires. If orch session end is never run, TimeInSession grows unbounded (currently showing 689h). Add auto-expiration: if no spawns for N hours, mark session inactive. Or reset on next session start. The 689h display makes orch status metrics meaningless.

title: [orch-knowledge] feature-impl: Apply the 13 stale CLI reference fixes to the o...
Infrastructure escape hatch overrides user config -- config.yaml backend: opencode ignored when isInfrastructureWork matches keyword in task
description: orch-go-1020 fixed explicit CLI flags being overridden. But user config (backend: opencode) sits below escape hatch in priority chain. Fix: treat user config backend as explicit intent, or kill the escape hatch entirely. The keyword list is also too broad -- skillc, dashboard, status.go, main.go all trigger it.

title: Audit: trace every code path from config.yaml to spawn execution — find all points where user config backend/model is overridden or ignored
description: Dylan set backend: opencode and default_model: codex in ~/.orch/config.yaml. All morning, spawns have been going to claude or failing. We found and fixed the infrastructure escape hatch (orch-go-1045), but there are likely more override points. This audit traces every path from config load to actual spawn to find them all.

title: Project config default spawn_mode overrides user backend when spawn_mode unset
description: When a project has .orch/config.yaml without spawn_mode, ApplyDefaults sets SpawnMode=opencode and DetermineSpawnBackend prioritizes it over user config backend. This can override an explicit user backend preference.

title: Warn on malformed userconfig during spawn (default_model/backend ignored)
description: Spawn pipeline ignores userconfig.Load errors, so malformed ~/.orch/config.yaml silently disables default_model/backend preferences without warning.

title: Treat user default_model as explicit for backend selection
description: DetermineSpawnBackend only considers --model flag as explicit. When default_model is set in user config (but spawnModel is empty), infra detection can force claude and ignore the configured model.

title: Investigation: skillc deploy pipeline is a source of chronic rework and confusion — audit source/compiled/deployed file relationships and fix
description: Repeated pattern: skill fixes get applied to the wrong file, deployed to the wrong location, or deployed correctly but not picked up by running sessions. This has caused at least 3 rounds of rework on the orchestrator skill alone (orch-go-1036, orch-go-1037, orch-go-1044). The problems: (1) Multiple copies of the same skill exist across different paths — source template, compiled intermediate, deployed copy, and sometimes stale copies in other directories. Nobody knows which file is authoritative. (2) Workers edit deployed artifacts instead of source templates because the relationship is unclear. skillc deploy then overwrites their work. (3) skillc deploy --target deploys to a root SKILL.md, not into the meta/orchestrator/ subdirectory where the old manual copy lives, creating two competing files. (4) Running sessions cache the skill at load time — deploying a fix has no effect until session restart, but there is no signal that this is the case. (5) orch-go-1015 (P3, open) already identified 5 stale orchestrator skill copies. The investigation should map the full lifecycle: source template → skillc compile → skillc deploy → session load → runtime. Identify every point of confusion and propose a simplified pipeline.

title: Headless codex spawns silently die — session created but not visible in OpenCode, no error surfaced to user
description: This is THE blocker for codex adoption. Config is now respected (orch-go-1045), orchestrator skill knows codex (orch-go-1044), but spawns silently die. State.create rejected Promise caching was fixed (orch-go-1031) but sessions still vanish. Need to trace what happens between orch spawn returning a session ID and the session actually running. Possible causes: auth failure for codex model, project-scoped OpenCode auth missing for price-watch, session init timeout, or the session dying immediately after creation with no error propagation.

title: [orch-go] feature-impl: Fix worker spawn wrapper prompt so workers trea...
Verify orientation frame propagation
[orch-go] investigation: repro headless codex spawn
Headless spawn must verify session exists and surface prompt_async errors — fire-and-forget creates silent failures
description: Two fixes needed: (1) After CreateSession + SendMessageInDirectory, verify session exists by GET /session/:id with correct x-opencode-directory header. If session missing or errored, surface error to user instead of reporting success. (2) Dashboard session listing should pass x-opencode-directory or query all projects. See investigation: orch-go-1051 SYNTHESIS.md for full trace.

title: OpenCode boundary fix — TUI exits when current session is deleted
description: Implemented event-driven exit in TUI sync.tsx when session.deleted event is received for the current session. Defense-in-depth for zombie bun processes. Code in opencode repo (commit b80de167a). Investigation: .kb/investigations/2026-02-18-inv-opencode-boundary-fix-add-process.md

title: [orch-go] systematic-debugging: Fix GPT/codex agents not committing work or rep...
Rethink agent observability architecture — current multi-source discovery is unfixable
Delete dead agent registry and state.db — not written to, 6 days stale, causes drift
Fix cross-project agent visibility — getKBProjects() fails silently, beads enrichment uses wrong project context
description: Cross-repo price-watch agents reported as dead when running fine. Root causes: (1) getKBProjects() fails silently returning empty slice when kb CLI unavailable, (2) workspace cache TTL (30s) causes stale data during high spawn activity, (3) beads comments fetch doesn't use correct project context from beadsProjectDirs map. This is the immediate pain relief step before the full observability rewrite. See .kb/investigations/2026-02-18-design-agent-observability-rethink.md Q4 and Step 1.

title: Implement work-centric agent discovery — invert from infrastructure-first to beads-first
description:
Core observability rewrite. Current serve_agents.go discovers agents from infrastructure (OpenCode sessions, tmux windows) then reconciles 5 data sources to figure out what work they represent. This is backwards and the reconciliation is where every bug lives (~1400 lines).

New model: Start from beads issues (status=in_progress), find workspace by beads_id, get session_id from workspace, check OpenCode for liveness only. O(n) single-pass instead of O(n*m) reconciliation.

Combined approach from two architects:
- Architect A (Feb 14): Move state into OpenCode sessions — infrastructure half-built (session metadata API, AGENT_MANIFEST.json)
- Architect B (Feb 18): Invert discovery — start from beads (work), not sessions (infrastructure)

Key artifacts:
- .kb/investigations/2026-02-18-design-agent-observability-rethink.md
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md
- .orch/workspace/og-arch-strategic-redesign-agent-18feb-0d2d/SYNTHESIS.md

Depends on: orch-go-1059 (delete dead registry first), orch-go-1060 (cross-project fix informs rewrite)

title: Wire OpenCode session metadata into agent discovery — complete the half-built bridge from Feb 14 decision
description:
The Feb 14 decision (.kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md) designed a 3-step plan to move agent state into OpenCode sessions. The OpenCode fork already has the features built:
- Session metadata API (commit 36f084ca5) — store beads_id, workspace, tier
- Session TTL (commit f3c3865b8) — configurable expiry
- Worker detection (commits 459a1bfba, 0922edfe7)
- State.create cache fix (commit b6823f82b)

And orch-go spawn already calls CreateSession() with metadata and writes AGENT_MANIFEST.json.

But serve_agents.go never consumed any of this. After 1061 lands (beads-first discovery), the next step is wiring the OpenCode session metadata as the liveness/identity lookup instead of the current SSE polling and session list scanning.

Steps from the decision:
1. Wire GET /session/status endpoint (eliminates ~1,400 lines of SSE status parsing)
2. Read session metadata for beads_id/workspace/tier (eliminates ~800 lines of workspace cross-reference)
3. Use session TTL for cleanup (eliminates ~1,200 lines of cleanup code)

Key artifacts:
- .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-lifecycle-ownership-own-accept-build.md
- .kb/models/opencode-fork.md
- pkg/opencode/client.go — already has CreateSession with metadata

title: Add integration tests for beads-first agent discovery in serve_agents.go
description:
orch-go-1061 rewrote agent discovery to start from beads issues instead of scanning OpenCode sessions/tmux. Zero tests were written. This is the same pattern that caused the Feb 18 nightmare — code ships without tests, breaks weeks later, fix causes another break.

Test coverage needed for the new discovery path:

1. discoverAgentsFromWork / listInProgressIssues:
   - Single-project: returns in_progress issues, ignores open/closed
   - Cross-project: queries multiple project dirs, deduplicates
   - Empty project dirs: falls back to default ListOpenIssues
   - Error handling: one project fails, others still returned

2. handleAgents beads-first loop:
   - Workspace found: session_id, spawn_time, project_dir populated from workspace
   - Workspace not found: agent still returned with beads data only
   - Session found: status derived from activity (active/idle/dead thresholds)
   - Session not found: agent shows as dead
   - Cross-project: correct project_dir from workspace, not session.directory

3. Cache behavior:
   - projectDirsMatchForIDs: cache invalidates when project context changes
   - Beads cache keyed by project dir (from orch-go-1060)

4. Edge cases:
   - --no-track agents (no beads issue)
   - Completed agents with SYNTHESIS.md
   - Stale agent filtering (displayThreshold)
   - Agent with beads issue but no workspace yet (just spawned)

Key files:
- cmd/orch/serve_agents.go — the code to test
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_test.go — existing tests to extend
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_cache.go — cache logic
- cmd/orch/serve_agents_cache_test.go — existing cache tests

title: Extract serve_agents.go (1,644 lines) — split HTTP handlers, agent discovery, and SSE streaming into focused packages
Extract serve_agents_test.go (1,662 lines) — restructure tests to match extracted serve_agents packages
Dashboard doesn't show newly spawned OpenCode agents — orch status sees them but /api/agents doesn't
description: First agent spawned after the observability rewrite (issues 1058-1062) that replaced 5-source reconciliation with beads-first discovery. The rewrite is the likely regression area. serve_agents.go discovery pipeline starts from beads issues but apparently isn't finding the workspace or OpenCode session for this agent.

title: Architect: Design unified spawn config precedence model — 10 bugs from competing config sources
Add LoadWithMeta helpers to config and userconfig — track which YAML keys are explicitly set
description: Step 1 of spawn config precedence implementation (design: orch-go-1067). Parse YAML into struct + map[string]any to detect key presence vs defaults. Add config.LoadWithMeta(projectDir) and userconfig.LoadWithMeta(). No behavior change — just adds explicitness metadata alongside existing load paths.

title: Implement ResolvedSpawnSettings centralized resolver — single function for all spawn config resolution
description: Step 2 of spawn config precedence implementation (design: orch-go-1067). Create pkg/spawn/resolve.go with ResolvedSpawnSettings struct (Backend, Model, Tier, SpawnMode, MCP, Mode, Validation + Source per field). Single Resolve() function that takes CLI flags, beads labels, project config meta, user config meta, and returns resolved settings with provenance. Follows 6-layer precedence: CLI flags > beads labels > project config (explicit) > user config (explicit) > heuristics > hardcoded defaults.

title: Wire ResolvedSpawnSettings into spawn pipeline — replace DetermineSpawnBackend and ad-hoc resolution
description: Step 3 of spawn config precedence implementation (design: orch-go-1067). Update spawn_cmd.go, orch work, and daemon spawn paths to call the centralized Resolve() function instead of DetermineSpawnBackend/ResolveAndValidateModel. Demote infrastructure escape hatch to only apply when no explicit config exists. This is the step that actually fixes the bug classes — all 3 open bugs (1047, 1048, 1049) should be closeable after this.

title: Log resolved spawn settings + source in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md — make config resolution traceable
description: Step 4 of spawn config precedence implementation (design: orch-go-1067). Add a Config Resolution section to SPAWN_CONTEXT.md showing each resolved setting and its source (e.g. 'Backend: claude (source: cli-flag)', 'Model: opus (source: user-config)'). Makes future config bugs immediately diagnosable from the spawn context.

title: Add precedence table tests and regression tests for 10 spawn config bug classes
description: Step 5 of spawn config precedence implementation (design: orch-go-1067). Unit tests for the Resolve() function covering all 6 precedence layers. Regression tests for each of the 10 bug classes from the design doc. Test that explicit values always beat defaults, infra escape hatch never overrides explicit config, and model/backend compatibility errors are surfaced.

title: Dashboard returns zero active agents — listActiveIssues finds nothing despite 3 running agents visible in orch status
description: orch status shows 3 active agents but /api/agents returns zero active. The 1066 fix (expand listActiveIssues to include open+in_progress) either didn't take effect in the running serve binary, or listActiveIssues is failing silently. bd show confirms these issues are in_progress. Likely stale binary or new discovery bug in the beads-first pipeline.

title: Dashboard shows 238 dead agents that are just beads issues — listActiveIssues fetches all issues across all projects and treats them as agents
description: The 1073 fix (unlimited bd list) correctly fixed zero-active but now every beads issue across all projects appears as a dead agent. The dashboard needs to filter: only treat issues as agents if they have evidence of being spawned (workspace exists, session_id, daemon:ready-review label, or agent-related metadata). Currently listActiveIssues returns everything and the status logic defaults to dead when no session matches.

Add no-local-state architectural constraint to CLAUDE.md and principles.md

Add enforcement in spawn pipeline that errors if an Anthropic model is paired with OpenCode backend. Add user config setting (e.g. allow_anthropic_opencode: true) to override for users who want the old behavior. This should integrate with the ResolvedSpawnSettings resolver from orch-go-1069 — the model/backend compatibility check in resolve.go is the natural enforcement point.

Add SetSessionMetadata to OpenCode client (PATCH /session/:id with metadata)
Tmux backend: inject session metadata after session discovery
Work-graph UI: replace hierarchy connectors with flow connectors
Architect: extract daemon config into cohesive package
/api/agents returns closed/completed agents — filter not excluding closed beads issues

E2E test script for two-lane agent discovery
Remove orch:agent label on close — beads scan is O(historical) not O(active)
Performance invariance test: seed N completed agents, assert status time is constant
Workspace lookup is O(historical) — LookupManifestsByBeadsIDs reads every manifest
GetAllSessionStatus fetches all sessions — add filtered endpoint to OpenCode fork
File upstream OpenCode issues: Session TTL, Session Metadata API, Session State endpoint
description: Phase 3 of lifecycle ownership boundaries decision. Three OpenCode features that would eliminate ~40% of orch lifecycle code (~3,400 lines): 1) Session TTL with configurable expiry - eliminates ghost/phantom/orphan classes, 2) Session metadata API (store beads_id, workspace, tier) - eliminates registry, 3) Session state HTTP endpoint (busy/idle queryable) - simplifies status polling. See .kb/decisions/2026-02-13-lifecycle-ownership-boundaries.md

title: E2E lifecycle test: spawn→visible→complete→gone full flow
Model drift: Agent Lifecycle
description:
Model drift detected for domain "Agent Lifecycle".

title: Audit verification infrastructure: what exists, what works, where the gaps are

Hook-based orchestrator frame enforcement for Claude Code sessions
Design verification levels: vocabulary gap + anti-proliferation
description:
Design explicit verification levels that solve the orchestrator-human vocabulary gap ('what kind of verification does this need?') without repeating the gate proliferation failure (14 gates → flag combos → --force as default).

Three constraints from Dylan:
1. Verification level should be explicit per-spawn (declared) AND inferrable from work type (default). The level determines which gates fire — inverse of current 'all gates on, skip what doesn't apply.'
2. --explain and --verify should be orchestrator judgment calls, not mechanical checkboxes. The orchestrator decides 'I need to verify this behaviorally' as part of completion reasoning, not because a flag forced it.
3. Gate friction must stay below the --force threshold. We've lived through the failure mode: gates proliferate, each reasonable, aggregate friction makes --force the happy path, system collapses to theater.

Inputs:
- Audit: .kb/investigations/2026-02-20-audit-verification-infrastructure-end-to-end.md (14 gates inventory, spectrum gap analysis)
- Models: ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/verifiability-first-development.md, ~/orch-knowledge/kb/models/control-plane-bootstrap.md
- Anti-pattern evidence: completion gate proliferation history (ask about Phase 6→7 gate removal)

1. What data sources does the work-graph UI use to resolve agent assignment? (opencode sessions? beads? workspace files?)
2. When orch spawn --tmux creates an agent, what gets written that the UI could discover? (workspace metadata, beads issue update, registry?)

4. What's the right fix surface — should tmux spawns write more metadata, or should the UI query more sources?

- OUT: Actually fixing it (that's a follow-up feature-impl)

title: 1182 regression: dashboard oscillates between correct agent status and 'unassigned' on polling cycle
description:
FRAME: The tmux liveness check from orch-go-1182 is causing the dashboard to oscillate between correct and incorrect agent status on every polling cycle. This is worse than the original bug (consistently showing dead) because the UI is now unstable.

LIKELY CAUSE: The tmux liveness check (FindWindowByWorkspaceNameAllSessions) is intermittently failing or timing out, causing alternating responses from the /api/agents endpoint. When the check succeeds → correct status. When it fails/times out → falls back to 'unassigned'.

BLOCKS: orch-go-1182 should not be considered complete until this is fixed.

description:
FRAME: We just reverted two implementations (orch-go-1182/1183) because a worker jumped from investigation findings directly to code, violating the two-lane architectural decision. The hotspot gate fired and was overridden with --force-hotspot. This is a systemic gap — investigations discover what's wrong, but only architects can decide how to fix it within existing constraints.

EVIDENCE:
- orch-go-1182: Worker implemented tmux liveness check directly from 1177 investigation
- orch-go-1183: Second worker tried to fix 1182's regression with caching

Second invocation should either fail fast or kill the old one.

title: Architect: design project group model for orch ecosystem (parent workdirs, sibling kb context, cross-project spawn scope)
description:
Orch-go has 19 registered projects as a flat list with no grouping concept. Three converging needs require a project group model:

1. **Sibling kb context in spawn** — spawning into toolshed should surface price-watch kb artifacts automatically when the task is relevant. Currently only searches target project's .kb/. Need to know which projects are 'siblings' to search.

2. **Parent workdir for cross-repo orchestration** — decision (2026-02-25-cross-repo-orchestration-from-parent.md) says scs-special-projects is the coordination point for work projects. But orch-go doesn't know that toolshed/price-watch/specs-platform/sendassist are children of scs-special-projects.

3. **Spawn/daemon scoping** — the daemon polls ALL registered projects. When orchestrating from scs-special-projects, should it only see work projects? Account routing (personal vs work) also maps to project groups.

Current implicit groups (by path):
- Orch ecosystem (personal): orch-go, opencode, beads, kb-cli, kn, orch-cli, etc. Home: orch-go
- SCS work: toolshed, price-watch, specs-platform, sendassist, scs-slack. Parent: scs-special-projects  
- Other personal: blog, snap, dotfiles

Questions to answer:
- Should groups be explicit config or inferred from directory structure?
- Does the parent dir (scs-special-projects) need a special role, or is 'group' enough?
- How do groups affect: kb context search scope, daemon polling, dashboard filtering, spawn account selection?
- What's the minimal viable model that unblocks cross-project kb context without over-engineering?

Also create the initial ~/.orch/groups.yaml with orch (explicit, personal account) and scs (parent-inferred, work account) groups. Write tests. Then wire into kbcontext.go: replace OrchEcosystemRepos hardcode with GroupsForProject() filter, with fallback to hardcode if groups.yaml doesn't exist.

Architect: reconcile Claude Code auto-memory with kb-cli knowledge externalization
description: Claude Code has a new 'auto memory' feature (MEMORY.md at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/) where the agent writes self-notes that persist across sessions. We already have kb-cli for knowledge externalization (decisions, constraints, tried/failed, investigations, guides, probes). These overlap — both store learned patterns and preferences that persist across sessions. An architect should decide: (1) Should auto-memory be disabled entirely in favor of kb-cli? (2) Should each have a distinct lane (e.g., auto-memory for session-ephemeral context recovery, kb-cli for durable cross-agent knowledge)? (3) Does auto-memory's 200-line startup injection compete with or complement CLAUDE.md instructions? (4) Risk: auto-memory is per-user and invisible to spawned agents — kb-cli artifacts are discoverable by all agents via kb context. Is that a feature or a bug? Reference: the Knowledge Placement table in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md already defines where different knowledge types go, but doesn't account for auto-memory.

But the frame guard (gate-orchestrator-code-access.py) blocks reading .go/.ts/etc files. This creates a loop: agent tries to spot-check code → gets blocked → falls back to trusting orch complete automated checks → which is actually fine, but the skill doesn't say that. Fix: update the Gate 2 section to clarify that behavioral verification for orchestrators means (1) orch complete --verified automated gates, (2) SYNTHESIS.md review, (3) running user-facing features if applicable, (4) spawning a verification investigation if automated gates are insufficient. NOT reading source code. Related: orch-go-1238 (explanation style update to same skill).

title: Architect: design code review gate for agent completion pipeline
description:
Current completion review is a knowledge management ceremony (reconnection, model updates, probe verdicts) not a code quality gate. The only code quality checks are: tests pass, go build succeeds, accretion check. Nobody reads the diff. SYNTHESIS.md is the agent's self-report — like approving a PR by reading the description and seeing CI green.

Questions for architect:
1. Should we introduce a code-review skill/agent as part of the completion pipeline?
2. When should it trigger? Every completion? Only full-tier? Only hotspot areas? Only features/bugs (not investigations)?
3. What should it check? Architectural violations, security, edge cases, coupling, correctness vs intent?
4. How should the orchestrator consume review findings? Block completion? Advisory? Separate beads issue for fixes?
5. Cost/benefit: doubled token spend per completion vs catching bugs that spawn 3 more agents to fix later.
6. Should this replace or augment the current Gate 2 behavioral verification?

Related: orch-go-1246 (Gate 2 conflicts with frame guard). The current system has the orchestrator trying to spot-check code but being blocked by the frame guard. A dedicated code-review agent would resolve this tension — the orchestrator stays strategic, a worker does the actual review.

orch clean kills active Claude Code tmux agents when OpenCode has no sessions
description: orch clean's stale tmux window detection assumes tmux windows map to OpenCode sessions. But claude-mode agents run Claude Code CLI directly in tmux — they have no OpenCode session. When OpenCode server restarts (0 sessions) or for claude-mode agents, clean incorrectly classifies active windows as stale. The fix: clean should check if the tmux window's process is still running (pane PID alive), not just whether an OpenCode session exists. Or at minimum, only clean windows for opencode-mode agents.

Comprehensive orch-go codebase audit (Feb 2026)
description: Full codebase audit of orch-go covering architecture, code quality, performance, security, and test coverage. Significant changes since last audit (Jan 3): registry eliminated (pkg/registry/ deleted), clean simplified 7→3 flags, lifecycle ownership boundaries established (Own/Accept/Build), Phase 5 fork integration issues created, daemon improvements, hotspot warnings added. Prior audit: .kb/investigations/2026-01-03-audit-comprehensive-orch-go-bugs-reliability-architecture.md. Focus areas: dead code from registry removal, lifecycle code that Phase 5 will eliminate (~3,400 lines), bloated files (spawn_cmd.go 2320 lines, session.go 2166 lines, doctor.go 1912 lines, complete_cmd.go 1669 lines, status_cmd.go 1625 lines, serve_agents.go 1560 lines), test coverage gaps, error handling patterns, and architectural coherence after recent refactors.

title: Move groups.yaml default path from ~/.orch/ to ~/.kb/ with backward-compat fallback
Benchmark worker reliability across Claude Code, Codex/GPT-5.4, and fallback path on real orch-go tasks
description:
Question:
Which worker model/backend combinations are operationally viable for orch-go today, measured on real protocol-heavy work rather than provider marketing claims?

What's known:
- Claude Code remains the most proven worker path in orch-go.
- GPT-5.4/Codex now looks strategically attractive because subscription-backed programmatic access is available through third-party tooling, unlike Anthropic's Max subscription path.
- Prior non-Anthropic stall-rate findings are stale enough that they should not be extrapolated to current GPT-5.4 behavior.

What's not known:
- Completion rate, stall rate, phase-reporting compliance, and synthesis quality for GPT-5.4/Codex on real orch-go tasks.
- Whether a cheaper fallback path should remain in the routing set or just exist as manual escape hatch.

Constraints:
- Use real orch-go tasks across at least feature-impl and investigation-style work.
- Measure protocol compliance, not just task quality.
- Keep the output decision-oriented: which paths are safe as defaults, overflow routes, or manual-only.

Relevant prior artifacts:
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-23-inv-investigate-revisit-opencode-model-routing.md
- .kb/threads/2026-03-24-openclaw-migration-from-claude-code.md
- .kb/models/daemon-autonomous-operation/claims.yaml

Deliverable:
A recommendation matrix for worker routing by skill type, including go/no-go thresholds and the smallest next validation needed.
acceptance_criteria:
- Compare at least 3 worker paths, with Claude included as baseline
- Report completion/stall/protocol-compliance results on real tasks
- Recommend default, fallback, and manual-only worker routes
- Surface any blocker that makes backend migration premature

title: Agents claim Phase: Complete without writing SYNTHESIS.md — 3/3 completions failed synthesis gate today
description: Pattern observed 2026-02-28: all 3 Phase: Complete agents reviewed (orch-go-i9qr, orch-go-vem4, orch-go-amty) had no SYNTHESIS.md. All were abandoned because there was nothing to review. Agents are reaching Phase: Complete in their session without writing the required synthesis artifact. This wastes compute and blocks completion review. Possible causes: skill templates not enforcing SYNTHESIS.md write before phase declaration, agents skipping synthesis under token pressure, or phase detection is wrong (agents didnt actually declare Phase: Complete).

title: Orient throughput metrics are global, not project-scoped — events.jsonl has no project field
description: Orient shows completions/abandonments/avg-duration from ALL projects but git numstat is project-local. The mismatch is misleading. Root cause: events.jsonl is a global log with no project/workdir field, and ComputeThroughput does no filtering. session_id has a project prefix that could be used but isn't. This is likely an instance of a broader pattern: anything consuming events.jsonl is implicitly cross-project.

title: Briefs mark-as-read state lost on orch serve restart — needs persistence
description:
## Symptom
Dylan marks briefs as read in the dashboard. After orch serve restarts (auto-rebuild, orch-dashboard restart, make install), all briefs show as unread again.

## Root cause
briefReadState is an in-memory map[string]bool (serve_briefs.go:40). No persistence layer. Every restart resets it.

## Fix
Persist read state to a JSON file (e.g. .orch/briefs-read-state.json). Load on startup, write on mark-as-read. Same pattern as other dashboard state files.

## Constraint
Keep it simple — a flat JSON file is sufficient. This is UI-only state, not authoritative (orch complete remains the comprehension gate).

Daemon status always shows stalled: last_poll not updated after initial cycle
description: Daemon writes daemon-status.json but never updates last_poll after the first poll cycle. DetermineStatus() always sees stale last_poll and returns stalled. Fix: update last_poll in the status file after each poll cycle completes.

title: Audit entropy spiral for recoverable features by functional area
description:
## Context
Reverted orch-go to Jan 18 baseline (0bca3dec) after entropy spiral (1163 agent commits, 5.4M LOC churn). Evidence preserved on entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch. Some features built during the spiral were genuinely valuable and should be recovered.

## Approach
Diff Jan 18 (0bca3dec) vs entropy-spiral-feb2026 by functional area. For each area, identify:
- Net-new features (not just churn/refactor cycles)
- Whether the feature compiled/worked at HEAD of spiral branch
- Dependencies on other spiral-era code
- Recovery complexity (clean extract vs needs adaptation)

## Areas to audit
1. **Web UI** - /work-graph route, dashboard agent cards, vite config changes
2. **Spawn system** - Backend abstraction (pkg/spawn/backends/), new flags, spawn pipeline
3. **Completion pipeline** - Pipeline decomposition, skip config, retry logic, SkipConfig type
4. **Daemon** - ProcessedIssueCache, skill inference improvements, processed-cache
5. **Observability** - SSE improvements, attention system (pkg/attention/), churn metrics
6. **Knowledge system** - Models/probes Go code (already recovering separately), kb reflect
7. **CLI commands** - Any new subcommands that were useful (orch phase, orch lint, etc.)

## Deliverables
- Recovery priority list: which features to recover first based on value vs complexity
- For each recommended recovery: exact commits/files to cherry-pick
- Flag features that depend on each other (can't recover X without Y)

## Method
Use git diff, git log, and file analysis on the entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch.
Do NOT modify any code - this is a read-only audit.

title: Recover probe/model/investigation lifecycle decision from entropy spiral branch
description: Dylan's preferred design: 3+ investigations on the same domain warrant creating a model, and from then on probes are made against the model (not standalone investigations). This was a design or decision artifact on the entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch that was lost in the rollback. Agent should search entropy-spiral-feb2026 branch commits for this artifact and recover it. The current decision (2026-02-14-probe-as-universal-evidence-gathering-primitive.md) proposes retiring investigations entirely — Dylan's actual design preserves investigations as the entry point that graduate into models, with probes as the confirmatory mechanism against established models. Key distinction: investigations are for novel exploration, probes are for testing model claims — NOT collapsing both into probes.

## Context

Related issues (e.g., verifiability-first Phase 1→5) are scattered across the tree, interleaved with unrelated work. The existing grouping modes (priority, area, effort) don't address this.

## Task

Add a 'dep-chain' grouping mode that clusters connected issues:
1. Add 'dep-chain' to GroupByMode type in work-graph.ts
2. Implement clusterByDepChain() function:
   - Build adjacency graph from edges (blocks + parent-child)
   - Find connected components via BFS/DFS
   - Name each cluster by the root node's title (or first node alphabetically)
3. Add to GroupByDropdown options
4. Add to 'G' keyboard shortcut cycle
5. Within each cluster, sort by dependency order (leaf→root, or topological)

## Algorithm sketch
```typescript
function clusterByDepChain(nodes: TreeNode[], edges: GraphEdge[]): GroupSection[] {
  // Build adjacency list from edges
  // BFS from each unvisited node to find connected component
  // Return GroupSection per component, named by root issue
}
```

## Dependencies
- Depends on orch-go-pcyb (/api/beads/graph must provide edges)

## Files to modify
- web/src/lib/stores/work-graph.ts - add dep-chain to GroupByMode, implement clusterByDepChain
- web/src/lib/components/group-by-dropdown - add dep-chain option

Enrich orch debrief: What Changed from completion explains, What Happened with titles
description:
Three improvements to orch debrief output:

1. **What Changed**: Pull --explain text from agent.completed events in events.jsonl. Currently only scrapes .kb/ git additions, missing all strategic narrative. The explain text from orch complete --explain is the synthesis layer.

2. **What Happened**: Show issue titles and skills instead of just counts. '21 completed' tells you nothing — listing the actual work tells you what mattered.

3. **Health section**: Remove hardcoded ok/none/none — it's ceremony without signal. Re-add when there's real data to populate it.

Key files: cmd/orch/debrief_cmd.go, pkg/debrief/

title: Orchestrator agents use Task tool instead of orch spawn despite skill prohibition
description: Orchestrator skill explicitly prohibits Task tool for spawning workers ('NEVER Task tool — use orch spawn or bd create'). But orchestrator agents default to Task tool when asked to 'spawn an agent' because Claude Code's system prompt makes it available as a default affordance. The skill instruction loses to the system prompt signal. Fix: add a PreToolUse hook that blocks Task tool calls when CLAUDE_CONTEXT=orchestrator, or remove Task tool from orchestrator sessions entirely via --disallowed-tools.

title: Merge orch-knowledge repo into orch-go
description:
Merge orch-knowledge into orch-go to eliminate cross-repo friction for knowledge and skills.

CURRENT STATE:
- orch-knowledge (~/orch-knowledge): skill sources (skills/src/), global kb (~/.kb/ symlink), principles
- orch-go (~/Documents/personal/orch-go): CLI, dashboard, project .kb/ (30+ models, hundreds of investigations), .beads/
- ~/.kb/ symlink → orch-knowledge's kb directory

WHAT TO MERGE:
1. Skill sources: ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/ → orch-go/skills/src/
2. Global kb contents: ~/.kb/ contents → orch-go/.kb/ (merge with existing project kb)
3. Any orch-knowledge-specific CLAUDE.md content
4. Any orch-knowledge-specific .beads/ issues (audit, close stale, or migrate)

TOUCHPOINTS TO UPDATE:
- ~/.kb/ symlink → point to orch-go/.kb/

title: orch tree: health smell detection with warning badges on clusters
description:
Make orch tree a diagnostic tool by adding health smell detection to clusters.

CONTEXT: orch tree currently renders clusters but gives no signal about which ones need attention. Health smells make the tree a triage tool — the orchestrator sees at a glance where synthesis is needed, where decisions are unacted, where models are untested.

SCOPE:
1. Detect and badge these smells on cluster nodes:
   - ⚠ 15+ investigations without a decision or model = needs synthesis
   - ⚠ Decision without any spawned issues = not acted on
   - ⚠ Model without probes = untested model claims
2. Add --smells flag to filter tree to only clusters with health smells
3. Add smell counts to depth-2 cluster summary line:
   ├─◉ coaching-plugin (15 investigations, 0 decisions) ⚠ needs synthesis
4. Smells should also appear in --format json output

VISUAL FORMAT (from design doc):
├─◉ Entropy Spiral (4 investigations, 2 decisions, 5 issues)  ⚠ health smell
├─◉ Coaching Plugin (15 investigations, 0 decisions) ⚠ needs synthesis
├─◆ Completion Verification (31 investigations, 1 model, 0 probes) ⚠ untested model

DESIGN DOC: docs/designs/2026-02-14-knowledge-lineage-tree-visualization.md (health smells section, line 339)
EXISTING CODE: pkg/tree/ (Phase 1a + 1b renderer, cluster detection, KnowledgeNode struct)

VERIFICATION:
- orch tree --smells shows only clusters with health smell badges
- orch tree --depth 2 shows smell counts on cluster summary lines
- orch tree --format json includes smell metadata per cluster
- Smell thresholds: 15+ investigations/no decision, decision/no issues, model/no probes
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: Fix daemon duplicate spawn: issue spawned 10 times in 20 minutes while orchestrator was present
description:
LIVE INCIDENT (Feb 14): orch-go-w50 was spawned 10 times by the daemon in ~20 minutes. All 10 agents did the same work. Issue remained OPEN status with triage:ready label, so the daemon kept picking it up every poll cycle.

Evidence (from orch status --all):
  orch-go-rpj   Extract rework expla...   Complete     4m
  orch-go-12a   Extract rework expla...   Complete     29m
  orch-go-0b1   Extract rework expla...   Planning     1m  (still running at time of detection)
  orch-go-7q8   Extract rework expla...   Complete     18m
  orch-go-vxh   Extract rework expla...   Complete     10m
  orch-go-og3   Extract rework expla...   Complete     23m
  orch-go-bga   Extract rework expla...   Complete     7m
  orch-go-es6   Extract rework expla...   Complete     13m
  orch-go-as3   Extract rework expla...   Complete     25m
  orch-go-c91   Extract rework expla...   Complete     20m

Root cause: daemon dedup cache (TTL-based) is not preventing respawns. The daemon polls bd ready, sees orch-go-w50 is still open+triage:ready, and spawns again. Each spawn gets a unique workspace name so dedup doesnt match.

Cost: ~80K tokens wasted (10 agents x ~8K avg), ~2-3% weekly API budget burned for nothing.

Immediate fix applied: bd update orch-go-w50 --status in_progress (stops daemon from picking it up).

Required fix: daemon must track which beads IDs it has already spawned agents for and refuse to spawn duplicates. The SpawnedIssueTracker already exists but clearly isnt working. This is the same bug documented in .kb/models/daemon-autonomous-operation/probes/2026-02-14-daemon-duplicate-spawn-ttl-fragility.md

This is P0 because it actively burns API budget and creates confusion every time the daemon runs.

title: Emit gate events from daemon-level enforcement — concurrency, ratelimit, governance gates are working but invisible to harness audit
description:
Harness audit reports 0 fires for concurrency, ratelimit, and governance gates because they enforce at the daemon layer (worker pool, RateLimiter, governance.go) without emitting spawn.gate_decision events. The audit only counts events in the spawn pipeline.

Fix: emit spawn.gate_decision events from:
1. pkg/daemon/pool.go — when spawn blocked by MaxAgents capacity
2. pkg/daemon/rate_limiter.go — when spawn blocked by MaxPerHour limit  
3. pkg/orch/governance.go — when governance warning is triggered

This closes the observability gap where enforcement happens but measurement can't see it. Phase 2 epistemic integrity: if a gate enforces, the audit should know.
