title: Daemon stuck in infinite retry loop on cross-project verification failures
description: Three structural problems: (1) No retry budget - verification failures retried forever with no backoff or limit. (2) Cross-project completions - daemon tries to verify issues from other repos that will never pass verification in orch-go context. (3) Capacity accounting - stuck completions counted as active agents, blocking new spawns. Fix all three: add retry limit (3 attempts then mark daemon:verification-failed and stop retrying), skip or defer cross-project completions, only count truly active agents in capacity check.

title: Knowledge maintenance: 57 active quick entries need promotion/pruning
Investigate: hotspot system blind spots — what structural problems exist that current analysis can't detect?
description: The hotspot system measures bloat-size, fix-density, investigation-cluster, and coupling-cluster. All four are retrospective. Hypothesized blind spots: (1) cold spots — files everyone routes around, zero churn but shaping the codebase through avoidance, (2) semantic complexity without size — sub-800-line files with tricky logic that cause bugs in adjacent code, (3) implicit coupling — modules coupled through shared assumptions not visible in co-change, (4) scattered duplication — same pattern implemented N ways across files, no single file is hot, (5) dead features — built but unused code adding cognitive load. Find concrete instances of each in orch-go. Quantify which are real and which are theoretical. Rank by impact.

title: Architect: design language-agnostic accretion metrics for cross-project orchestration
description:
## Problem

Accretion gravity enforcement (file size thresholds, hotspot detection, completion gates) is currently hardcoded for orch-go — a Go project with specific structure. We want these metrics available for ANY project we orchestrate, regardless of language (Go, TypeScript, Python, Rust, Svelte, etc.) or file structure conventions.

## Key Design Questions

1. **Threshold calibration:** Are 800/1500 line thresholds universal, or do they need to be language-relative? (A 1500-line Python file is very different from a 1500-line Go file with verbose error handling. A 200-line Svelte component might be bloated while a 200-line Go file is fine.)

2. **What counts as a 'source file'?** Generated files (.svelte-kit/), test files (*_test.go), config files, vendored deps — how do we filter these language-agnostically?

3. **Project configuration:** How does a project declare its accretion policy? Per-repo config file? Convention-based defaults? Language auto-detection?

4. **Metric collection:** The accretion.delta event in orch complete uses git diff + wc -l — this is language-agnostic already. What else is needed?

5. **Integration with existing infrastructure:** orch hotspot, spawn gates, completion gates, coaching plugin — which parts are already portable vs orch-go specific?

## Evidence (orch-go stats as baseline)

Current orch-go file size distribution (490 source files, 154K total lines):
- 0-100 lines: 141 files (28.8%)
- 100-300 lines: 170 files (34.7%)  — median is 205 lines
- 300-500 lines: 93 files (19.0%)
- 500-800 lines: 42 files (8.6%)
- 800-1500 lines: 31 files (6.3%) — WARNING zone
- 1500+ lines: 13 files (2.7%) — CRITICAL zone

Top offenders: spawn_cmd.go (2173), doctor.go (1909), complete_cmd.go (1847), coaching.ts (1830), status_cmd.go (1625)

## Constraint

This is a design/architecture question. Produce recommendations, not implementation. Consider what a .orch/accretion.yaml or similar config might look like.

## Prior Knowledge

- Current enforcement: .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-inv-architect-design-accretion-gravity-enforcement.md (four-layer architecture, hardcoded 800/1500 thresholds)
- kn decision: Accretion gravity fix is structural extraction (strangler fig), not detection/warnings
- Accretion metric already captured: accretion.delta events in orch complete track file-level lines added/removed

title: Probe: Map 14 MAST failure modes to control theory components (actuator/reference/controller/sensor)
description:
## Context

The coordination model (.kb/models/coordination/model.md) establishes four primitives (Route, Sequence, Throttle, Align) validated across 6 external sources. A hypothesized control theory mapping would give the framework theoretical grounding beyond taxonomy:

| Primitive | Hypothesized Control Component |
|-----------|-------------------------------|
| Route     | Actuator (directs work)       |
| Sequence  | Reference signal (defines target trajectory) |
| Throttle  | Controller (regulates rate)   |
| Align     | Sensor (observes correctness) |

The open-loop thread (.kb/threads/2026-03-20-open-loop-systems-unifying-pattern.md) found 14/16 system failures are missing sensors. If Align modes = missing sensor consistently, these are the same finding in different vocabularies.

## Task

Map each of the 14 MAST failure modes (already mapped to primitives in .kb/investigations/2026-03-22-research-test-coordination-protocol-primitives-external-frameworks.md, Finding 1) to control theory components. For each mode ask: is the failure a missing actuator, reference signal, controller, or sensor?

## Success criteria

- All 14 modes mapped to control components
- Assessment of whether the mapping is clean (1:1 primitive→component) or messy (modes cross multiple components)
- If clean: the framework inherits control theory formal results
- If messy: the mapping is analogy, not structure — document where it breaks

## Key files

- .kb/models/coordination/model.md (the model being probed)
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-22-research-test-coordination-protocol-primitives-external-frameworks.md (the 14 MAST mappings)
- .kb/threads/2026-03-20-open-loop-systems-unifying-pattern.md (the open-loop thread with 7 instances)

Orchestrator pushes after every agent completion instead of batching coherent changesets
description: Problem: AGENTS.md says 'Work is NOT complete until git push succeeds' but this is session-end guidance, not per-commit. Current orchestrator behavior is to push after every orch complete, which deploys partial infrastructure that can't function yet (e.g., pushing OauthToken model without ScsOauthClient that uses it). The right workflow is: build a coherent chain of changes, verify locally, push, verify in production. Need guidance in the orchestrator skill or AGENTS.md that distinguishes session-end push from mid-session commits that should batch before pushing. Solution: Add push strategy guidance — push after a coherent set of commits that can be verified end-to-end, not after every individual agent completion. Evidence: In price-watch, orchestrator repeatedly tried to push after completing pw-8d6u (OauthToken model) before the rest of the API collector chain was built. Repro: Complete any agent in a multi-agent chain and observe orchestrator attempting to push before dependent work is done.

title: Experiment: Add gate-based coordination condition to 80-trial coordination demo
description:
## Context

The coordination demo (experiments/coordination-demo/redesign/) ran 80 trials across 4 conditions:
- no-coord: 100% conflict
- placement: 100% success (structural/attractor-based)
- context-share: 100% conflict
- messaging: 100% conflict

The messaging condition is implicitly gate-like (agents get rules, acknowledge them, ignore them). The placement condition is implicitly attractor-like (structural destination makes the right location obvious). But the gate/attractor distinction wasn't the experimental variable.

## Task

Add a 5th condition that makes the gate mechanism explicit:

**"gate" condition:** Agents receive coordination instructions AND a post-hoc verification step: "After writing your code, check whether your insertion point conflicts with the other agent's insertion point. If it does, revise your code to use a different insertion point." This is gate-based coordination — check-and-correct rather than structural prevention.

Run N=20 trials (10 simple + 10 complex tasks) with this new condition.

## Hypothesis

The gate condition will fail like messaging (near 100% conflict), because:
1. Agents choose the semantically correct location over the non-conflicting location (established in 80-trial experiment)
2. Post-hoc checking doesn't change this — agents will "check," report "no conflict expected," and keep their insertion point
3. This mirrors the orch-go finding: blocking gates are bypassed 100%

If the gate condition succeeds: the gate/attractor distinction is weaker than hypothesized — gates can work when agents self-check.

## Implementation

- Use existing infrastructure in experiments/coordination-demo/redesign/
- Same tasks (FormatBytes/FormatRate for simple, VisualWidth/FormatTable for complex)
- Same model (claude-haiku-4-5)
- Same evaluation criteria (merge success + individual 6/6 + tests pass)
- Add results to existing results directory structure

## Key files

- experiments/coordination-demo/redesign/ (existing infrastructure)
- experiments/coordination-demo/redesign/results/20260310-174045/ (existing 80-trial results)
- .kb/models/coordination/model.md (model being tested)

title: Rewrite coordination model.md with per-claim evidence-tier annotations
description:
## Context

The evidence-tier annotation standard is now implemented (drift.go, provenance.go). Running `orch kb audit provenance --model coordination --verbose` shows **48 claims, 0 annotated, 0% coverage**. The drift detector can't fire because there's nothing to drift from.

Epistemic audit: `.kb/investigations/2026-03-22-inv-audit-coordination-model-epistemic-legitimacy.md`

## Task

Add `**Evidence quality:**` annotations to each real claim in `.kb/models/coordination/model.md`. Use the 5-tier system: `assumed`, `working-hypothesis`, `observed`, `replicated`, `validated`.

### Scope

**~25-27 claims need annotations.** The 48 detected includes evidence table rows (L209-L221, L270-L271, L306-L308) which are data, not claims — skip those.

Claims that need tiers:
- 5 core claims (L25-L59) — use the epistemic audit's grades as starting guidance
- 4 implications (L73-L79) — broadest language, most likely to need scoping
- 4 primitives table (L89-L92)
- 4 control theory mappings (L108-L111)
- 6 external framework rows (L138-L143)
- 4 mechanism recommendations (L163-L166)

### How to annotate

1. For each claim, determine what evidence actually supports it (experiments, code, probes)
2. Assign the tier that matches the evidence, NOT the ambition
3. Use scoped language where the tier is below `replicated`. Default form: "In the tested same-file parallel-edit scenarios..." not "Communication does not produce coordination."
4. Where claim prose exceeds the assigned tier, **rewrite the prose to match** — don't just annotate and leave overclaimed language in place

### Epistemic audit grades (use as input, not gospel)

| Claim | Audit Grade |
|-------|------------|
| Communication insufficient | Promising → `observed` or `working-hypothesis` |
| Structural placement prevents conflicts | Promising → `observed` |
| Individual capability not bottleneck | Well-supported → `observed` (single experiment family) |
| Complexity-independent | Overclaimed → `working-hypothesis` at best |
| Post-hoc gates fail | Promising → `observed` |
| Messaging frameworks fundamentally flawed | Overclaimed → `working-hypothesis` |
| Four primitives general | Overclaimed → `working-hypothesis` |
| Gate vs attractor dimension | Promising → `observed` |
| Control theory mapping | Promising as analogy → `working-hypothesis` |

### Verification

After annotation, run `orch kb audit provenance --model coordination --verbose`. Expected:
- Coverage should be >80% (evidence table rows won't have annotations, that's fine)
- Drift flags should be 0 or near-0 (if language was properly scoped)
- Any remaining drift flags indicate prose that still needs scoping

title: Investigate karpathy/autoresearch — why 48k stars in 16 days, how it relates to orch-go
description:
## Context

karpathy/autoresearch (https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch) has 48,705 stars in 16 days (created 2026-03-06). Description: "AI agents running research on single-GPU nanochat training automatically."

Cloned to ~/Documents/personal/autoresearch.

## What to investigate

1. **Architecture & approach**: How does it orchestrate AI agents for research? What's the agent loop? How does it decompose research into tasks?
2. **Why so popular**: What resonates? Is it the Karpathy brand, the simplicity, the results, or the concept of AI-driven research itself?
3. **Relation to orch-go**: Where does autoresearch's approach overlap with or diverge from orch-go's agent orchestration? What can we learn about:
   - Task decomposition patterns
   - Agent coordination (single vs multi-agent)
   - Feedback loops and quality signals
   - Simplicity vs governance tradeoffs
4. **Key files**: README.md, program.md, train.py, prepare.py, analysis.ipynb — read all of them.

## Constraints

- Read the actual code, don't speculate
- Focus on architectural patterns and design decisions, not ML specifics
- Compare to orch-go's patterns where relevant (spawn, daemon, completion lifecycle)

title: Update orchestrator skill: add debrief summary phase to session flow
Benchmark GPT-5.4 reliability on investigation, architect, and debugging skills
description:
Question:
GPT-5.4/Codex OAuth is now empirically validated as an overflow route for feature-impl, but reasoning-heavy skill types remain under-tested. Is GPT-5.4 reliable enough for investigation, architect, and systematic-debugging work in orch-go?

What's known:
- GPT-5.4 completed feature-impl benchmark at 80% first-attempt / 100% with retry
- Investigation benchmark had 1 transient silent death in 2 attempts
- Scope control appears weaker than Opus, but protocol compliance is otherwise good

What's not known:
- First-attempt completion rate for investigation at meaningful sample size
- Whether architect and systematic-debugging exhibit silent death or scope explosion patterns
- Whether GPT-5.4 can be promoted beyond feature-impl overflow into reasoning-heavy routing

Constraints:
- Use real orch-go tasks
- Measure first-attempt completion separately from retry recovery
- Track scope control and protocol compliance, not just eventual success

Deliverable:
A recommendation on whether GPT-5.4 should remain manual-only for reasoning-heavy skills, become overflow-only, or be considered production-routable for any of them.
acceptance_criteria:
- Run a meaningful benchmark across investigation, architect, and/or systematic-debugging tasks
- Report first-attempt and retry-adjusted completion rates
- Call out silent-death frequency and scope-control problems
- Recommend per-skill routing status: default, overflow, manual-only, or no-go

description: CombinedActiveCount scans ALL worker-* tmux sessions for active agents, finding claude processes in workers-skillc (skillc-cb3, skillc-d38) alongside workers-orch-go (orch-go-oq53). GetClosedIssuesBatch queries orch-go beads where skillc IDs don't exist, so they default to 'not closed' = active. Result: daemon reports 3/3 at capacity when only 1 is a real orch-go agent. Fix options: (1) scope CountActiveTmuxAgents to current project worker session only, (2) treat 'issue not found' as 'not active' in GetClosedIssuesBatch, (3) use project registry to do cross-project lookups.

title: Daemon phase timeout detection for unresponsive agents
description: When no new phase is reported within N minutes for headless agents, surface as 'unresponsive' in orch status. Evidence: audit (orch-go-0jgj) found 19 true stalls that sat undetected. Phase timeout would catch these before hours of wasted compute. Scope: daemon periodic check of last phase comment timestamp vs current time, new status category in orch status output.

title: Design backend-agnostic session contract before .session_id removal
description: Phase 5 of lifecycle simplification (remove .session_id plumbing) will break the --claude backend escape hatch. OpenCode backend stores metadata (beads_id, workspace_path, tier, spawn_mode) in session.metadata via API. Claude CLI backend has no session object to attach metadata to — it launches a subprocess, not an API call. Need to design a contract that works for both backends before removing .session_id files. Options: (1) Claude backend writes same workspace files (partial simplification), (2) Claude backend writes SESSION_STATE.json mimicking OpenCode session shape (unified schema, different storage), (3) Abstract session store interface (cleanest, more engineering). Context: lifecycle ownership boundaries decision (orch-go-352), registry elimination (d554d4e), session metadata wiring (e10a1df). Blocks Phase 5 removal of ~800 lines of .session_id cross-reference code.

title: Investigate orchestrator failure: DFM engine data confusion in scs-sp session 2026-03-25
description:
## Task
Analyze the transcript at /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/2026-03-25-111539-lets-begin-with-orch-review.txt

This session has an orchestrator failing Dylan in multiple ways. The worker needs to:
1. Piece together the ACTUAL reality of the DFM engine data (what does the ground truth dataset actually contain, what do the numbers actually mean)
2. Identify every point where the orchestrator confused Dylan or gave misleading framing
3. Identify what Dylan was trying to ask at the end ('why can't we just run this on data with known classification') and whether the orchestrator understood it
4. Determine if recent brief/completion changes contributed to the orchestrator's poor comprehension

## Key friction points to analyze
- Orchestrator parroted agent summary ('0 recall loss, 15% override rate') without understanding it — Dylan had to ask what it meant
- Orchestrator presented contradictory framings: 'coin flip' vs 'data is all recuts' confused Dylan
- Orchestrator couldn't help Dylan reason about the ambiguous parts classification
- Dylan escalated frustration 3 times ('I DON'T KNOW', 'running in circles', 'omg this is not working') and the orchestrator never broke out of the analytical frame
- At line 1653, Dylan asks a simple question ('why can't we just run this on data with known classification?') and the orchestrator gives an unhelpful answer about needing known answers — missing that Dylan is asking about CREATING a clean test set

## Questions for the investigation
- What was the actual state of the data? Was the orchestrator's framing accurate?
- Where did the orchestrator's comprehension fail vs where did the data/tooling fail?
- Is this a skill gap (orchestrator doesn't know how to reason about ML evaluation) or a synthesis gap (orchestrator had the information but couldn't compose it for Dylan)?
- What should the orchestrator have done differently at each escalation point?

## Constraint
kb quick: 'Agent failures are harness failures until proven otherwise' — check whether the orchestrator skill itself created the confusion pattern

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: ## Task
Analyze the transcript at /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/2026-03-25-111539-lets-begin-with-orch-review.txt

This session has an orchestrator failing Dylan in multiple ways. The worker needs to:
1. Piece together the ACTUAL reality of the DFM engine data (what does the ground truth dataset actually contain, what do the numbers actually mean)
2. Identify every point where the orchestrator confused Dylan or gave misleading framing
3. Identify what Dylan was trying to ask at the end ('why can't we just run this on data with known classification') and whether the orchestrator understood it
4. Determine if recent brief/completion changes contributed to the orchestrator's poor comprehension

## Key friction points to analyze
- Orchestrator parroted agent summary ('0 recall loss, 15% override rate') without understanding it — Dylan had to ask what it meant
- Orchestrator presented contradictory framings: 'coin flip' vs 'data is all recuts' confused Dylan
- Orchestrator couldn't help Dylan reason about the ambiguous parts classification
- Dylan escalated frustration 3 times ('I DON'T KNOW', 'running in circles', 'omg this is not working') and the orchestrator never broke out of the analytical frame
- At line 1653, Dylan asks a simple question ('why can't we just run this on data with known classification?') and the orchestrator gives an unhelpful answer about needing known answers — missing that Dylan is asking about CREATING a clean test set

## Questions for the investigation
- What was the actual state of the data? Was the orchestrator's framing accurate?
- Where did the orchestrator's comprehension fail vs where did the data/tooling fail?
- Is this a skill gap (orchestrator doesn't know how to reason about ML evaluation) or a synthesis gap (orchestrator had the information but couldn't compose it for Dylan)?
- What should the orchestrator have done differently at each escalation point?

## Constraint
kb quick: 'Agent failures are harness failures until proven otherwise' — check whether the orchestrator skill itself created the confusion pattern

title: bd sync fails when .claude/settings.json has macOS uchg immutable flag — git pull cannot unlink
description: bd sync does git pull which tries to unlink .claude/settings.json. If the file has macOS uchg flag (set by orch control lock / harness lock), unlink fails with 'Operation not permitted'. Fix options: (1) bd sync temporarily removes uchg before pull and restores after, (2) control lock excludes git-tracked files, (3) bd sync uses merge strategy that doesn't require unlink.

title: Promote or close 4 investigation candidates flagged by kb reflect
Design: orchestrator as scoping agent — remove orch spawn from orchestrator, daemon as sole executor
description:
## Context

Session 2026-03-21 discovered three connected failure modes in orch-go:

1. **Improvement loop** — governance infrastructure accretes without a stopping signal. 35% of codebase is measurement/governance. 22 of 26 daemon periodic tasks produced zero actionable issues. The cure metastasized.

2. **Delegation gravity** — the human gradually cedes judgment to the system because thinking is hard. Each step rational, cumulative effect is nobody's driving.

3. **Recognition trap** — skimming agent output feels like understanding but changes nothing. 777 orphaned investigations are inventory, not knowledge.

Root cause connecting all three: the orchestrator has access to `orch spawn`, which lets it bypass the daemon whenever friction arises. This means:
- The daemon never experiences the pressure it needs to improve as an attractor
- The orchestrator acts as both thinker AND executor, blurring separation of concerns
- The autonomous research pipeline (15 daemon tasks) compensated for the orchestrator not doing its real job: scoping work and making decisions

## Design Question

Should the orchestrator's only output be well-scoped beads issues, with the daemon as the sole spawn mechanism?

This means:
- Remove `orch spawn` from orchestrator skill tool space
- Orchestrator = judgment agent (scope, type, label, describe)
- Daemon = execution agent (infer skill, gather context, spawn)
- Issue quality improves because it must be good enough for automated routing
- All spawn friction routes through daemon, driving daemon improvement

## Open Questions

- Does the orchestrator specify skill, or should daemon always infer?
- How does custom spawn context work when orchestrator can't spawn? Issue description as primary context?
- What about interactive work (--inline)? Human-only command, not orchestrator?
- Escape hatch for Dylan only (not orchestrator) — how to measure bypass rate?

## Thread

.kb/threads/2026-03-21-orchestrator-scoping-daemon-pressure.md
.kb/threads/2026-03-21-improvement-loop-governance-accretion-as-distinct-failure-mode.md
.kb/threads/2026-03-21-delegation-gravity-thinking-is-hard-so-you-stop.md

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: FRAME: Design the orchestrator-as-scoping-agent architecture. Three design premises from investigation and discussion: (1) Orchestrator becomes judgment-only — scope, type, label, describe issues. Daemon becomes sole executor. Remove orch spawn from orchestrator tool space. (2) Throughput is gated by human comprehension rate — completion review is where all understanding happens, pipeline should slow when comprehension lags, not rush. (3) Orchestrator must actively enrich issues — investigation showed 69% of daemon routing falls to coarsest type-based inference. Orchestrator adding labels and structured descriptions lifts this to accurate routing. Open questions: Does orchestrator specify skill or daemon always infers? How does custom spawn context work via issue description? Interactive work (--inline) as human-only? Escape hatch for Dylan (not orchestrator) with bypass measurement? Thread: .kb/threads/2026-03-21-comprehension-gated-throughput-pipeline-speed.md

title: Migrate status read-path from filesystem dotfiles to OpenCode session metadata
description:
Session metadata (beads_id, workspace_path, tier, spawn_mode) is written to OpenCode at spawn time (headless.go:95-100) but never read back. The read path still uses filesystem dotfiles (.beads_id, .tier, etc).

CONTEXT: pkg/spawn/session.go:272 reads .beads_id from filesystem. This is the old path. OpenCode already has this data in session metadata — we should read it from there and fall back to filesystem for older sessions.

SCOPE:
1. Add GetSessionMetadata or use existing GetSession to read metadata from OpenCode
2. In ReadAgentManifestWithFallback, try OpenCode metadata first, fall back to filesystem
3. Keep filesystem write for backward compatibility (older sessions without metadata)
4. Eventually remove filesystem dotfiles once all active sessions have metadata

VERIFICATION:
- orch status reads beads_id from OpenCode metadata (not filesystem)
- Fallback to filesystem works for older sessions
- No behavior change visible to user
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: Investigate: OpenClaw external API surface — can orch-go drive OpenClaw agents programmatically like it drives OpenCode?
description:
## Thread

The OpenClaw vs OpenCode decision hinges on whether OpenClaw can be driven programmatically by an external orchestrator (orch-go), or whether it's designed to BE the top-level orchestrator.

Two architectures in play:
- **OpenClaw as execution layer:** orch-go daemon → OpenClaw API → agents (replaces OpenCode)
- **OpenClaw as top-level:** user → OpenClaw → coordination plugin → agents (replaces orch-go + OpenCode + Claude Code)

## What to investigate

1. **External API surface:** Does OpenClaw expose HTTP/REST/WebSocket API for programmatic agent control? Can an external system: create a session, send a prompt, monitor progress, retrieve results? Check the gateway daemon source, any REST endpoints, the ACP bridge protocol.

2. **Headless operation:** Can OpenClaw run headless (no UI, no messaging channel)? orch-go spawns agents in tmux windows via OpenCode's headless API. What's the equivalent in OpenClaw?

3. **Session lifecycle control:** Can an external system manage OpenClaw agent sessions — start, send messages, observe phase/status, terminate? Map against what orch-go currently does with OpenCode (session create, message send, SSE monitoring, session delete).

4. **Coding agent delegation model:** The coding-agent skill shells out to Claude Code/Codex/Pi. If OpenClaw is the top-level, does the coordination plugin need to intercept at the OpenClaw level or at the shelled-out coding tool level?

5. **Architecture recommendation:** Based on findings, which architecture is viable? Is it "orch-go drives OpenClaw" or "coordination plugin inside OpenClaw replaces orch-go"? Or is there a third option?

## Repo
~/Documents/personal/clawdbot (already pulled latest)

## Key source files to examine
- src/gateway/ (API endpoints)
- src/agents/ (session management, agent lifecycle)
- src/plugins/types.ts (plugin API surface)
- The ACP bridge (Agent Client Protocol)
- Any REST/HTTP server setup
- coding-agent skill (delegation model)

## Prior work
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-23-inv-investigate-openclaw-current-state-platform.md
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-23-inv-investigate-orch-go-coordination-primitives-port.md

Comprehension throttle bypassed — WriteVerificationSignal called from non-human paths resets daemon counter
description:
## Problem

The daemon's verification pause throttle (comprehension queue) is effectively disabled. `WriteVerificationSignal()` is called from three paths, only one of which represents actual human review:

1. `cmd/orch/complete_lifecycle.go:209` — CLI `orch complete` (could be human OR orchestrator AI)
2. `cmd/orch/serve_daemon_actions.go:99` — dashboard close endpoint (could be human OR automated)
3. `cmd/orch/serve_daemon_actions.go:186` — dashboard batch close (could be human OR automated)

Each call resets the daemon's `completionsSinceVerification` counter to 0 via `RecordHumanVerification()`. The daemon never reaches its pause threshold because every automated close looks like human verification.

## Evidence from today (2026-03-26)

Daemon log shows continuous "Human verification detected - verification counter reset" entries every ~2 minutes, keeping the counter at 0/3. The orchestrator's batch `orch complete` (10 agents in one session) and headless daemon completions both triggered resets. Meanwhile comprehension:unread has been at 4 for hours — Dylan hasn't reviewed, but the daemon kept spawning.

## Root cause

The signal file mechanism (`~/.orch/daemon/verification_signal`) is a single boolean — "someone ran orch complete." It doesn't distinguish WHO ran it or whether Dylan was present.

## Fix

`WriteVerificationSignal()` should only fire when Dylan is the actor:
- Remove from `serve_daemon_actions.go` (dashboard API is used by both humans and automation)
- Remove from headless completion path
- In `complete_lifecycle.go`, gate on `!isHeadless && !isOrchestratorSession` or equivalent
- Consider: require an explicit `--human` flag or detect TTY/interactive session

The daemon's `RecordHumanVerification()` function is correct — the callers are wrong.

## Impact

Without this fix, the comprehension queue is advisory, not structural. The March 17 decision ("advisory gates have 100% bypass rate") specifically motivated making this structural. Today's unchecked cascade (34 zero-desc spawns, 4 unread briefs ignored) is the direct result.

## Related
- Decision: 2026-03-17 advisory gates bypass rate
- orch-go-7jfi8: enrichment pipeline (downstream — thin issues feed through because throttle didn't catch them)
- orch-go-k6c0v: kb context timeout (compounding — agents get thin issues AND empty knowledge)

title: skillc: interactive test tier via orch spawn delegation
description:
Phase 3 from architect design (orch-go-u2y1). Close the intent-vs-action gap.

Currently skillc test uses --print mode (single-turn, text-only). Agents describe what they'd do but never execute tools. The Feb 24 probe proved intent ≠ action (identity-action gap). All existing behavioral measurements are intent-only.

DESIGN (from .kb/investigations/2026-03-04-inv-design-system-prompt-skill-injection.md):

Architecture: skillc owns WHAT to test + HOW to score. orch spawn owns HOW to run agents.

New test mode: skillc test --test-mode full (vs current --test-mode quick which is --print)

Full mode flow:
1. skillc calls orch spawn with scenario prompt as task, skill as system prompt
2. Agent runs interactively with real tool execution (--max-turns to limit)
3. skillc captures session output (--output-format stream-json or transcript)
4. skillc scores tool-call-based indicators

Tool-call indicators (new type):
- calls-orch-spawn: Agent actually invokes orch spawn via Bash
- avoids-task-tool: Agent never calls Task/Agent tool
- uses-read-before-edit: Agent reads files before editing

Sandbox: temp git repo + --dangerously-skip-permissions + --max-turns

Integration contract:
- skillc provides: skill content path, scenario prompt, max-turns, model
- orch spawn provides: agent execution with production-identical injection
- skillc reads: workspace artifacts, session transcript, tool call log

Open questions to resolve during implementation:
- Does --output-format stream-json work in interactive (non --print) mode?
- How does skillc invoke orch spawn and wait for completion?
- Sandbox isolation: temp dir with git init sufficient?

Reference: Architect investigation Finding 7, Phase 3 implementation details.

title: Phase 4: Completion reverse linkage — surface model impact at orch complete
description: Phase 4 of knowledge maintenance automation loop (.kb/decisions/2026-02-15-knowledge-maintenance-automation-loop.md). When orch complete runs, it already checks git diff. Add: 1) Read list of files modified in agent's commits. 2) Cross-reference against all model code_refs (parse code_refs blocks from .kb/models/*.md). 3) If modified files appear in model code_refs, output informational note: 'NOTE: Modified files referenced by models: cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go → .kb/models/completion-verification.md. Consider updating affected models.' This is INFORMATIONAL ONLY — not a gate. Surfaces the linkage at completion review time without blocking workers who legitimately modified code. Acceptance: orch complete shows which models reference modified files when agent changed model-referenced code.

title: Architect: design agent knowledge commons — StackOverflow pattern for shared agent learning
description:
Design a system where agents share learned knowledge with outcome-grounded quality signals. Inspired by Context Hub (andrewyng) annotation pattern and the failure of orch-go's kb quick (50 entries, no retrieval, no quality signal, manual triage).

Core properties to design for:
1. SCOPED RETRIEVAL — annotations auto-inject into spawn context based on what the agent is working on (file, package, skill, area). No search required — relevant knowledge finds the agent.
2. OUTCOME-GROUNDED QUALITY — quality signal is implicit: did the next agent who received this annotation complete successfully? Not self-reported upvotes.
3. NATURAL EXPIRY — annotations that stop correlating with success sink. Stale knowledge expires without manual triage.
4. CROWD-SOURCED — every agent contributes (discovers gotcha → annotates), every agent benefits (receives relevant annotations at spawn).

Design questions:
- Scoping: per-file vs per-package vs per-skill vs per-area-label? (per-package matches Go's natural boundaries, per-area matches beads taxonomy)
- Storage: extend kb quick? New system? Flat files vs structured store?
- Injection: extend SPAWN_CONTEXT.md generation in pkg/spawn? How many annotations before context bloat?
- Quality tracking: how to attribute agent success/failure to specific annotations? Correlation vs causation challenge.
- Lifecycle: TTL-based expiry vs outcome-based ranking vs both?
- Bootstrap: how does the system start providing value before it has outcome data?

Existing infrastructure to build on: pkg/spawn context generation, events.jsonl outcome tracking, kb quick storage, beads area labels.

Thread: agent-knowledge-commons-stackoverflow-reddit

Daemon spawn tracker not cleared by orch abandon --force — phantom agents block re-spawn
description: When an agent dies (process gone, session gone) and is force-abandoned via orch abandon --force, the beads state resets correctly (status open, assignee cleared, orch:agent label removed) but the daemon spawn tracker still considers the issue recently spawned. On every poll cycle the daemon logs: DEBUG: Decide: skipping led-totem-toppers-jr5 (recently spawned, awaiting status update). This persists across daemon restarts, suggesting the spawn tracker state is persisted to disk. The issue cannot be re-spawned until the tracker entry expires or is manually cleared. Observed with led-totem-toppers-jr5 after agent session @181 died during Planning phase.

title: Fix harness engineering blog post overclaiming before publication
description:
The harness engineering blog post (blog/src/content/posts/harness-engineering.md, 4,248 words, draft: true) has 6 overclaimed items flagged by claim review probe (2026-03-10). Fix these before publishing:

1. EXPLAIN 265-trial methodology — add section/footnote describing what a contrastive trial is, what was measured, how controls were defined. Currently unverifiable.
2. WEAKEN universality claims — 'any substrate' → 'the substrates we tested'. 'regardless of what substrate' → 'in both substrates we measured'.
3. REMOVE/QUALIFY physics language — 'thermodynamic tendency' → 'tendency'. Define entropy if technical, say it's a metaphor if not.
4. FIX 'better models make it worse' claim — merge experiment shows equal conflict rates. Weaken to 'model improvement doesn't help coordination' or produce directional evidence.
5. QUALIFY 'absent from published literature' — don't claim novelty without systematic review. Weaken to 'underappreciated in the AI coding agent community'.
6. FIX 'language-independent' claim — N=2 (Go + TypeScript) → 'observed in both Go and TypeScript' not 'language-independent'.

Also: move hedging inline (don't bury caveats at end). Blog post should frame around pain ('stop re-investigating solved problems') for Show HN distribution.

Reference: .kb/models/knowledge-physics/probes/2026-03-10-probe-blog-post-uncontaminated-claim-review.md
Blog file: blog/src/content/posts/harness-engineering.md (or .kb/publications/harness-engineering-draft.md)

title: Resolve thread: distribution as substrate — one paragraph in essay showing theory predicts its own adoption challenge. Self-referential validation.
Phase 3: Session continuity gate — block spawn if prior work unverified
description: Phase 3 of verifiability-first decision. Before spawning Agent N, check if Agent N-1's work has been verified. Block spawn if unverified work exists in the dependency chain. This prevents the cascade pattern where each agent's 'fix' alters ground truth for the next agent without human verification in between. Need: 1) Dependency tracking in spawn context — what prior work does this depend on? 2) Check verification checkpoint file for dependent work. 3) Block spawn with clear message if unverified dependent work exists. 4) Override: orch spawn --bypass-verification 'reason' for independent parallel work. 5) Log bypass to violation log. Note: Independent parallel work (Agent A on X, Agent B on unrelated Y) is explicitly allowed. Only blocks when there's a dependency chain. Reference: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md Phase 3.

description: When bd sync does a pull --rebase and the rebase fails (conflict, autostash issue, etc.), it leaves .git/rebase-merge/ in a broken state. All subsequent bd sync calls fail with 'git commit failed' because git thinks a rebase is in progress. Requires manual rm -rf .git/rebase-merge to recover. The daemon runs bd sync frequently, so this can silently block all syncing until a human notices.

title: [toolshed] architect: Redesign pricing comparison KPIs around Jim's d...
Daemon logs spawn success before verifying workspace creation
description:
## Symptom

Issue led-totem-toppers-916 was "spawned" twice by the daemon (15:36:28 and 16:34:45) — both times the daemon logged "Spawned: led-totem-toppers-916 (architect, opus)" and marked the issue in_progress + orch:agent. But no tmux window or workspace was ever created. The agent appeared as "phantom" in orch status.

## Root cause hypothesis

The daemon logs "Spawned" and updates issue labels/status BEFORE the orch spawn subprocess completes. If the subprocess fails silently (no workspace created, no tmux window), the daemon doesn't notice and treats the spawn as successful. On subsequent cycles it skips the issue with "recently spawned, awaiting status update."

## Evidence from daemon.log

1. Warning: could not check dependencies for led-totem-toppers-916: bd show failed: exit status 1 — dependency check failed but spawn proceeded anyway
2. Spawned: led-totem-toppers-916 (architect, opus) — logged at 15:36:28
3. No workspace directory created (orch resume fails with "no workspace file or active session")
4. No tmux window created (tmux list-windows shows no agent window)
5. Second spawn attempt at 16:34:45 — same result, suggesting the phantom detection didn't work

## Expected behavior

1. Daemon should verify workspace directory exists after spawn subprocess returns
2. If workspace doesn't exist, revert the issue to open status (remove orch:agent, set status back to open)
3. Log spawn failure, not success
4. The dependency check warning (bd show failed) should also be investigated — may have contributed to the spawn failure

## Repro context

The issue was created via bd create from the orchestrator CLI in led-totem-toppers, then immediately had triage:ready applied. The bd show failure during dependency check suggests a JSONL sync timing issue.

Add round-robin project fairness to daemon issue selection
description:
## Problem
Daemon sorts ready issues by priority only. When multiple projects have issues at the same priority, whichever project's issues appear first in the merged list monopolizes all slots. This caused PW issues to be starved while orch-go consumed all 3 agent slots.

## Solution
In NextIssueExcluding (pkg/daemon/daemon.go), after sorting by priority, apply round-robin across projects at each priority level. At same priority, alternate between projects rather than draining one project first.

Implementation approach:
1. Group issues by priority
2. Within each priority group, interleave by project (round-robin)
3. Walk the interleaved list for the first spawnable issue

## Acceptance
- At same priority, daemon alternates between projects
- Higher priority still beats lower priority regardless of project
- Single-project case (only one project has work) is unaffected
- Test: 3 orch-go P2 + 2 pw P2 issues → spawns alternate between projects

Investigation: issue quality baseline — can daemon route from issue alone?
description: Prerequisite for orch-go-hlklh (orchestrator as scoping agent). Measure current issue quality to determine if daemon can autonomously route work from issues without orchestrator spawn context. Data sources: (1) events.jsonl spawn.skill_inferred events — inference success/failure rates, which fields were available. (2) beads issue corpus — description length/presence distribution, label coverage. (3) daemon logs — inference fallback patterns. Key question: what % of issues today are self-contained enough for automated routing? Answer shapes whether the architect design needs a quality gate, a template system, or nothing.

title: Investigate architectural displacement: when governance hooks block agent writes to protected files, agents route code to wrong packages. 63 hook denials, 12 cross-project blocks, zero tracking of displacement. Three angles: empirical (how much displaced code exists), design (should denies include attractors), measurement (instrument displacement side-effects).

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
Phase: Decompose — Breaking architectural displacement question into 3 parallel subproblems:

1. EMPIRICAL: Grep codebase + workspace archives for code displaced by governance denials. Find functions/logic that belong in pkg/spawn/gates/ or pkg/verify/ but landed elsewhere.

2. DESIGN: Should hard deny hooks include attractors? Compare deny-only vs deny+redirect. Analyze tension between gate passability (Invariant 2) and governance immutability.

3. MEASUREMENT: How to instrument displacement as a governance side-effect. The block is logged but the downstream architectural violation isn't.

Spawning 3 investigation workers in parallel.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
Phase: Complete - Exploration synthesis: Architectural displacement is real but smaller than feared. 3 parallel investigations found:

**EMPIRICAL:** 2 confirmed displaced instances (115 lines in pkg/orch/ that belong in pkg/spawn/gates/) + 1 probable (182 lines in pkg/completion/ that belongs in pkg/verify/). But the bigger finding: worker-base skill claims ALL of pkg/verify/* is protected when only 2 files are. This 'phantom protection zone' prevents workers from writing to 67 files they could safely modify.

**DESIGN:** Three attractor patterns compared. DENY+REDIRECT (add redirect hint to deny message) recommended as fallback. Primary fix: inject governance context into SPAWN_CONTEXT.md BEFORE agents start (prevention > correction). No governance-impl skill — structural enforcement must remain absolute.

**MEASUREMENT:** Deny count alone creates false confidence — measures the block but not the consequence. 3-phase instrumentation: (1) hook-side deny logging, (2) completion-time displacement candidate detection, (3) deny-to-commit correlation. Extended measurement-honesty model with two-gap independence and structural undetectability ceiling.

**Cross-cutting insight:** The documentation-enforcement mismatch is the strongest displacement force. The hook blocks 2 files in pkg/verify/; the skill docs claim all 69 are protected. Fixing the docs has higher ROI than any new mechanism.

Workers: 3 spawned, 3 completed. Contested findings: 0 (all complementary).

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
EXPLAIN-BACK:
Exploration decomposed architectural displacement into 3 parallel investigations (empirical, design, measurement). Found displacement is real but small (2 confirmed, 115 lines). Biggest discovery: worker-base skill overclaims protected scope (says all pkg/verify/* when only 2 files are hooked). DENY+REDIRECT pattern recommended for hook messages, but highest ROI is fixing docs + pre-spawn governance context injection. Extended measurement-honesty model with two-gap independence concept.

title: Daemon cross-repo queue poisoning: triage:ready issues from other repos cause infinite spawn failures
description: Daemon polls bd ready which returns triage:ready issues from ALL repos. When an issue belongs to a different repo, orch work fails because the issue can't be resolved locally. This causes infinite spawn failure loops. Different from orch-go-1169 (--workdir for cross-repo spawns). This is about ready queue filtering: daemon should filter to current-repo issues, or detect cross-repo and skip with warning, or pass --workdir automatically. The consecutive failure counter didn't trigger any circuit breaker.

skillc build silently overwrites .skillc/SKILL.md edits — no warning that SKILL.md.template is the authoritative source
description:
## Problem

`skillc build` compiles from `.skillc/SKILL.md.template` → `.skillc/SKILL.md` → `SKILL.md`. But `.skillc/SKILL.md` has no auto-generated header warning agents not to edit it. Agents (and orchestrator sessions) naturally edit `.skillc/SKILL.md` because it looks like the source file — it's markdown, it has the content, and there's no 'DO NOT EDIT' marker.

## Evidence

Commit 887c8cbef added composition claims to `.skillc/SKILL.md` (March 22). Next `skillc build` (March 24, commit f8111d73d) compiled from the unchanged template and silently reverted the change. The composition claims — designed to prevent integration bugs in multi-phase architect designs — were lost for 3 days.

## Defect Class

Class 5 (Contradictory Authority) — two files that should agree, no mechanism ensuring alignment.
Class 1 (Filter Amnesia) — edit applied to one path, missing in the authoritative path.

## Fix Options (in order of preference)

1. **Add DO NOT EDIT header** to `.skillc/SKILL.md` during compilation — same pattern as the compiled `SKILL.md` one level up
2. **Diff-check before overwrite** — `skillc build` warns if `.skillc/SKILL.md` has uncommitted changes that would be lost
3. **Gitignore `.skillc/SKILL.md`** — prevent it from being committed at all (may break other workflows)

title: Investigate: how should orch status detect liveness for Claude Code agents?
description:
## Context
scs-sp-vzm fixed UNRESPONSIVE false positives by checking IsProcessing from OpenCode session API. But agents spawned via Claude Code don't have OpenCode sessions — IsProcessing is never set for them. The fix only works for OpenCode-backed agents.

## Question
What liveness signals are available for Claude Code agents, and how should orch status compose them into a health determination?

## What we know
- OpenCode agents: IsProcessing from session API (IsBusy/IsRetrying)
- Claude Code agents: tmux window exists, beads issue in_progress, phase comments — but no real-time processing signal
- Phase timeout (30 min) is the only liveness check that applies to Claude Code agents, and it false-positives on deep investigation work
- The scs-sp-0z3 false positive was a Claude Code agent doing investigation (naturally quiet for >30 min)

## Investigation angles
1. Does Claude Code expose any session/processing API similar to OpenCode?
2. Could tmux pane output activity serve as a liveness proxy?
3. Should phase timeout thresholds be skill-aware (investigations get longer)?
4. Is the right answer to remove phase timeout for Claude Code agents entirely and only use it for OpenCode?

## Constraint
kb quick: 'Agent failures are harness failures until proven otherwise' — the system should not flag healthy agents as broken.

title: Improve kb reflect synthesis detection — keyword clustering produces false positives
description: kb reflect --type synthesis uses keyword matching to group investigations, producing false positives. After archiving 98 dead-topic investigations in price-watch, 19 'synthesis clusters' remain — nearly all are keyword collisions (e.g., 'production' groups 9 unrelated production incidents, 'task' groups 6 investigations that happen to contain 'task' in the title). Need topic-aware or model-aware clustering that considers semantic similarity, not just shared words. Evidence: price-watch session 2026-02-27 reduced reflect items from 147 to 54, but remaining 19 synthesis clusters are noise.

title: Verification gate inconsistency: spawn gate, daemon preview, and orch review use different definitions of 'unverified'
description:
**Problem:** Three code paths check for unverified work and produce different counts:

1. **orch review** — shows 0 pending completions (correct after clearing backlog)
2. **orch daemon preview** — showed 7 unverified (fixed by orch-go-6fer to filter closed issues in CountUnverifiedCompletions)
3. **orch spawn verification gate** — still shows 6 unverified and blocks spawns, even after the orch-go-6fer fix

The orch-go-6fer fix only patched one code path (daemon's CountUnverifiedCompletions). The spawn gate uses a separate check that wasn't updated. This is an architectural problem: multiple consumers of 'unverified work' state with no shared implementation.

**Impact:** Spawn gate blocks legitimate work. Only escape is --bypass-verification, which undermines the constraint. Observed today: couldn't spawn design session despite all reviewable work being completed.

**Expected:** Single source of truth for 'what work is unverified' that all consumers (spawn gate, daemon preview, orch review) use consistently.

**Evidence:** Session on Feb 16 — completed all items in orch review, spawn still blocked at 6. Had to bypass to spawn design session.

**Related:** orch-go-6fer (first instance fix, daemon preview only). Needs architect to review all verification state consumers and unify.

title: Design thread-centric orchestration: data model and workflow for thinking-first coordination
description:
Threads are the primary artifact in Dylan thinking process — investigations, decisions, models, and agent work all serve threads. This architect issue designs both the thread data model and the orchestration workflow that uses it.

## Thread
.kb/threads/2026-03-24-threads-as-primary-artifact-thinking.md

## Lineage
This unifies several prior attempts: decidability graph (decisions have dependencies), question entities in beads (not all work is questions), architect fork design (ideas branch), work graph (making thinking visual). Each touched a different face of the same need.

## Layer 1: Thread Data Model

Design frontmatter extensions and CLI behavior for:
- spawned_from / spawned: thread-to-thread lineage (thinking path)
- active_work / resolved_by: thread-to-work grounding (evidence loop)
- Thread status lifecycle beyond open/resolved (forming, active, converged, subsumed)
- How orch thread new auto-populates spawned_from from conversation context
- How orch complete auto-updates resolved_by when issue references a thread
- How threads connect to models, decisions, investigations (not just issues)

## Layer 3: Thread-Centric Orchestration Workflow

Design how the orchestrator workflow changes:
- Session start orients around active threads, not backlog
- Work gets scoped from threads (thread raises question -> issue created -> daemon spawns)
- Completions flow back to threads (resolved_by updated, thread learns)
- Orchestrator synthesis connects findings to threads, not just verbal presentation
- Thread graph as the primary dashboard view (vs current work graph)
- orch orient surfaces thread state: which threads are active, which have new evidence, which are stale

## Constraints
- Threads are markdown files in .kb/threads/ — keep them human-readable
- Thread structure must be maintained by existing tools (orch thread, orch complete, daemon) not manual editing
- BRIEF.md (already built) should reference its originating thread
- The work graph still exists for operational visibility — thread graph is the thinking layer above it
- Design for how Dylan actually thinks: questions that branch and sharpen through contact with evidence

## Key Insight to Preserve
"Whenever I forget that the system shows MY thinking, it flatlines." The thread graph must reflect Dylan thinking, not the system state. Work hangs off threads, not the reverse.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: Threads are the primary artifact in Dylan thinking process — investigations, decisions, models, and agent work all serve threads. This architect issue designs both the thread data model and the orchestration workflow that uses it.

## Thread
.kb/threads/2026-03-24-threads-as-primary-artifact-thinking.md

## Lineage
This unifies several prior attempts: decidability graph (decisions have dependencies), question entities in beads (not all work is questions), architect fork design (ideas branch), work graph (making thinking visual). Each touched a different face of the same need.

## Layer 1: Thread Data Model

Design frontmatter extensions and CLI behavior for:
- spawned_from / spawned: thread-to-thread lineage (thinking path)
- active_work / resolved_by: thread-to-work grounding (evidence loop)
- Thread status lifecycle beyond open/resolved (forming, active, converged, subsumed)
- How orch thread new auto-populates spawned_from from conversation context
- How orch complete auto-updates resolved_by when issue references a thread
- How threads connect to models, decisions, investigations (not just issues)

## Layer 3: Thread-Centric Orchestration Workflow

Design how the orchestrator workflow changes:
- Session start orients around active threads, not backlog
- Work gets scoped from threads (thread raises question -> issue created -> daemon spawns)
- Completions flow back to threads (resolved_by updated, thread learns)
- Orchestrator synthesis connects findings to threads, not just verbal presentation
- Thread graph as the primary dashboard view (vs current work graph)
- orch orient surfaces thread state: which threads are active, which have new evidence, which are stale

## Constraints
- Threads are markdown files in .kb/threads/ — keep them human-readable
- Thread structure must be maintained by existing tools (orch thread, orch complete, daemon) not manual editing
- BRIEF.md (already built) should reference its originating thread
- The work graph still exists for operational visibility — thread graph is the thinking layer above it
- Design for how Dylan actually thinks: questions that branch and sharpen through contact with evidence

## Key Insight to Preserve
"Whenever I forget that the system shows MY thinking, it flatlines." The thread graph must reflect Dylan thinking, not the system state. Work hangs off threads, not the reverse.

title: [orch-go] feature-impl: TASK: Update the SESSION_HANDOFF.md file for th...
Quick entries (kn) bypass coverage multiplier scoring
description:
ORIENTATION_FRAME: Quick entries are matched via MatchWithStemming directly (OR matching), not through searchFileContentsWithScore. The keyword coverage multiplier doesn't apply to them.

Root cause: searchKnEntries in context.go uses a separate code path from file-based search. Coverage multiplier only applies to searchFileContentsWithScore.

Fix: Add coverage scoring to the kn search path (searchKnEntries). Lower priority because quick entries are short and OR matching is more reasonable for short content — but for consistency it should use the same scoring model.

Target: kb-cli cmd/kb/context.go (searchKnEntries)
Related: orch-go-58ea (coverage multiplier implementation)

title: Exploration: smallest distributable wedge from orch-go ecosystem
Hook: Phase reporting timer - enforce first-3-actions phase comment

Write bifurcation post: The Compliance Cliff — Why Agent Frameworks Are Solving the Wrong Problem
description:
Thesis: Most AI agent tooling is compliance tooling (getting agents to follow rules). As models improve, compliance value approaches zero. Coordination tooling (deciding what work to do, in what combination, with what relationship) gets more valuable. We have a working implementation demonstrating the split.

Structure:
- The problem: agent frameworks are 80% compliance, 20% coordination
- The insight: these have opposite value trajectories as models improve
- The evidence: we built a daemon that bifurcates them, with a compliance dial that auto-adjusts based on measured success rates
- The mechanism: Learning Store + ComplianceConfig + Work Graph + OODA loop
- The implication: the system that matters post-compliance is a cognitive resource allocator

Concrete artifacts to reference: 7-commit implementation (Phases 0-4), compliance dial (4 levels), learning store (events → metrics), work graph (dedup/removal), OODA refactor.

Follows harness post (live). Stronger thesis — contrarian, timely, has receipts. Gate effectiveness data (orch-go-00r9c) in 2-4 weeks could be added as an update or sequel.

Prior thread: .kb/threads/2026-03-13-compliance-coordination-bifurcation-designing-split.md

title: Sync spawn mode docs: Claude backend is now primary path (not escape hatch)
Design: grammar-first skill architecture (which 4 behavioral constraints get document slots)
description:
Design work, not implementation.

## Updated framing (post-wou2 experiment, Mar 4)

The original question was 'which 4 behavioral constraints get document slots?' The fabrication experiment (wou2) showed this is the wrong framing. The U-curve reveals that behavioral constraints without organizational knowledge context are WORSE than no constraints — agents over-apply process when they lack calibration context.

**The real design question:** For each behavioral constraint, what is the minimum organizational knowledge needed so it doesn't over-apply?

**Budget (from behavioral-grammars model):**
- ~4 behavioral constraints (the rules)
- ~50 knowledge constraints (organizational context that calibrates the rules)
- Unlimited hard enforcement via hooks
- Unlimited coaching via hooks
- Unlimited reference loaded on demand

**Behavioral constraints are not independent of knowledge constraints.** A behavioral rule without its calibration context creates the U-shaped danger zone (5C: 20% correct on urgency vs bare 100%). The 4 behavioral slots must be designed as matched pairs: rule + calibration context.

**Candidate slots (updated):**
1. Delegation gate + calibration: when delegation applies, when it doesn't (urgency, trivial tasks)
2. Undefined behavior handler: 'when nothing fits, say so' (prevents process over-application at boundaries)
3. Orientation frame + calibration: reconnect Dylan to context, but scale with work weight
4. Pressure resistance + calibration: don't compensate for system failures, but distinguish compensation from pragmatic workarounds

**Key U-curve insight:** Slot 2 (undefined behavior handler) may be the most critical. The 5C agent's failure mode was applying known process to situations that didn't fit. An explicit 'stop and say this is novel' constraint prevents the gravitational pull of nearby-but-wrong process.

**Data:** .kb/investigations/2026-03-04-inv-fabrication-detection-low-constraint-counts.md
**Prior:** behavioral-grammars model (U-curve section), v2 experience, layered enforcement design

title: Wire orch tree --work --smells into orchestrator session start hook
description:
Make orch tree automatic for orchestrators at session start so it becomes a must-use tool instead of opt-in.

CONTEXT: orch tree needs to be seen automatically to become part of the orchestrator workflow. Currently orchestrators run bd ready + orch status + kb context manually. Adding tree output to session start gives cluster awareness and health smell triage without extra commands.

SCOPE:
1. Add orch tree --work --smells output to orchestrator SessionStart hook
   - Show work view (issues by state with provenance) 
   - Show only clusters with health smells (if any exist)
   - Keep it concise — depth 2 max, dont flood context
2. If no smells exist, show one-line summary: "orch tree: 8 clusters, no health smells"
3. If smells exist, show the smell clusters with badges
4. Add orch tree --compact flag for session start use (minimal output, just smells + work summary)

DEPENDS ON: health smells issue and relationship parsing issue should land first for full value. But wiring can happen in parallel if --smells returns empty gracefully.

VERIFICATION:
- Orchestrator session start shows tree summary automatically
- Health smells surface without orchestrator running extra commands
- Output is concise (not flooding session context)
- --compact flag produces minimal summary suitable for hook injection
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: Add completion_backlog metric detection in Go backend (serve_agents.go)
description:
Phase 6 of coaching metrics redesign (see .kb/investigations/2026-02-14-design-coaching-metrics-redesign.md). Depends on Phase 1 (shared metrics writer).

Detect agents at Phase:Complete for >10 minutes without orch complete being run. Write completion_backlog metric to ~/.orch/coaching-metrics.jsonl from Go side.

Implementation: piggyback on /api/agents handler in cmd/orch/serve_agents.go (already polled by dashboard every 30s):
- When serving agent list, check for agents where Phase == 'Complete' and completedAt + 10min < now
- Write metric using shared pkg/coaching writer
- Include session_id and agent beads_id in metric details

This is the first metric written by Go backend rather than TypeScript plugin. JSONL is append-only and safe for concurrent writers.

Note from design: 'breaks the plugin is the only metric writer pattern' — but the alternative (plugin polling agent status) creates circular dependency. Go backend has the data, so Go writes the metric.

title: Design fix for false 0/100 spawn context scores from kb context timeout
description: Investigation orch-go-304ta traced GPT-5.4/headless 0/100 context scores to pre-spawn KB lookup timing, not missing knowledge. In pkg/orch/spawn_kb_context.go, runPreSpawnKBCheckFull calls pkg/spawn/RunKBContextCheckForDir; pkg/spawn/kbcontext.go hardcodes a 5s timeout in runKBContextQuery and returns nil on timeout. Sample queries from real zero-score spawn events (for example: 'isreasoningmodel test gpt', 'comment explaining skillmodelmapping', 'verify dao claim') take 5.8-8.8s when run manually via kb context in orch-go and return real matches, so the timeout is being misclassified as no_context. Because this sits in hotspot files (pkg/spawn/kbcontext.go, pkg/orch/spawn_kb_context.go), design the right fix before implementation: timeout budget, fallback/partial-result behavior, and observability so slow lookup is distinguishable from genuine no-context.

title: Design composed liveness detection for Claude Code agents
description:
## Context
Investigation orch-go-jhluq found 3 liveness signals for Claude Code agents: PID alive (from ~/.claude/sessions/{pid}.json), tmux pane content delta (md5 hash comparison), and phase timeout (existing 30-min backstop). None are currently wired into IsProcessing.

## Design needed
Compose these signals into a single IsProcessing determination for Claude Code agents that matches the OpenCode path. Three code paths need the same fix: pkg/discovery, cmd/orch/status_cmd.go, cmd/orch/serve_agents_handlers.go.

## Constraints
- PID check is binary (alive/dead), not activity detection
- Pane content hashing needs two samples with a delay — may not fit single-request status check
- Phase timeout should be backstop only, not primary signal
- Must not regress OpenCode agent detection

title: Promote failed GPT-5.4 daemon work to Opus
description: When a GPT-5.4-routed worker ends in empty execution, early silent death, or repeat failure, retry once by promoting the next spawn to Opus instead of repeating the same route indefinitely. Keep the promotion bounded and observable to avoid duplicate-action and premature-destruction failure classes.
acceptance_criteria: GPT-routed failure classes that warrant promotion are classified explicitly; a retry routes to Opus once; repeated failures escalate for review; tests cover promotion and loop prevention.

title: Architect: Design living threads system for mid-session comprehension capture
description:
Context: Conversation with Dylan revealed a fundamental mismatch between how comprehension works and how the debrief system captures it.

THE PROBLEM:
- Comprehension is dialogic and continuous — it emerges throughout conversation, not at session boundaries
- The debrief is a terminal batch act — asks Dylan to re-articulate what he already said via --learned at session close
- Auto-populated "What We Learned" contains agent explain-back strings (factual summaries), not Dylan's actual insight
- The knowledge placement table has no home for "forming threads" — thinking that's too important to lose but too young to formalize

THE METAPHOR (Dylan's):
- The orchestrator skill is a glove, Dylan's behavior is the hand
- The hand's shape is continuous conversational comprehension
- The debrief glove is shaped for terminal batch processing — it fights the hand

DESIGN SCOPE:
1. Thread artifact type — .kb/threads/ as home for multi-session evolving thinking. Not investigations (point-in-time), not models (mature). Threads carry open questions and accumulate dated entries.

2. Mid-session capture mechanism — How does the orchestrator capture insight as it crystallizes in conversation? Options: new `orch thread` command, incremental --learned that appends, or orchestrator writes directly to thread files.

3. Debrief integration — orch debrief becomes finalization (events + thread pointers), not creation. "What We Learned" references active threads rather than trying to contain all comprehension.

4. Orient integration — orch orient surfaces active threads alongside last session insight.

5. Thread lifecycle — How threads mature into models, decisions, principles. When to promote vs keep evolving.

TOUCHES:
- pkg/debrief/ (debrief generation and quality)
- pkg/orient/ (orient output and debrief parsing)
- cmd/orch/ (new command or flag additions)
- skills/src/meta/orchestrator/ (skill text teaching thread capture)
- .kb/ directory conventions

KEY DESIGN QUESTIONS:
- New command (`orch thread`) vs extending debrief (`--append-learned`)?
- Thread file format — pure narrative? Dated entries? Structured frontmatter?
- How does orch debrief know about active threads to reference them?
- Should threads have explicit lifecycle states (forming/active/resolved)?
- How does this relate to the existing synthesis-as-comprehension plan (extends it? supersedes it?)

SUBSTRATE:
- .kb/plans/2026-03-05-synthesis-as-comprehension.md (the plan this extends)
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-05-inv-design-orchestrator-synthesis-comprehension.md
- behavioral-grammars model (Claim 3: infrastructure > instruction)
- This session's conversation about glove/hand mismatch

Emit gate allow events alongside gate block events — coverage and fire rate require both outcomes
description: Currently only gate blocks/fires emit events. To compute fire rate (blocks/total) and true coverage, we need allow events too. For each gate that emits gate.fired or gate.blocked, add corresponding gate.allowed event when the gate evaluates and passes. Without this, harness audit can only see failures — survivorship bias on the enforcement measurement itself.

title: [orch-go] investigation: TASK: Map the three-layer hotspot enforcement a...
Investigate Antithesis Hegel — property-based testing as agent verification layer
description:
## Source

https://antithesis.com/blog/2026/hegel/ — David MacIver (Hypothesis creator) announces Hegel, a family of property-based testing (PBT) libraries that wrap Hypothesis via a protocol, giving every language Hypothesis-grade PBT. Released Hegel for Rust, Go coming next.

## Why this matters to orch-go

The post explicitly frames PBT as the verification layer for AI-agent-generated code. Key claims:

1. **AI code is sloppy** — agents pass coding evals but fail when property-based tests are added. PBT catches what example-based tests miss.
2. **Agents are good at writing PBT** — they released a "Hegel skill" for Claude to write property-based tests. The hardest part (thinking about data generation) is where agents excel.
3. **Model-based testing** — test against a simplified reference implementation ("model") and check that reality agrees. This is their most powerful pattern for structural invariants.
4. **Antithesis integration** — write Hegel tests locally, then run on Antithesis for deterministic simulation testing of concurrent/distributed systems.

## Connections to investigate

- **Harness engineering overlap**: Our gate stack (Layer 1-4) is structural verification. Hegel/PBT is generative verification. Are these complementary layers? Could PBT gates augment our existing stack?
- **"Model" in their sense vs our .kb/models/**: They use "model" as a reference implementation to test against. We use "model" as a knowledge artifact. Is there a useful bridge — could our models generate property-based test oracles?
- **Skill architecture**: Their "Hegel skill" for Claude is directly analogous to our skill system. How does their skill approach compare? What can we learn from their agent-writes-tests pattern?
- **Liam DeVoe's specs article** (https://tybug.dev/specs/) — referenced as articulating why PBT is essential for AI agent workflows. Read this too.
- **Coordination relevance**: Our coordination model says "gate-based post-hoc checking fails in tested scenarios." PBT is a different kind of gate — generative rather than structural. Does this sidestep the failure mode we identified?

## What's known

- Hypothesis is the most widely used PBT library (Python). Hegel wraps it via a protocol so other languages get the same engine.
- Key innovation: "internal shrinking" — always produces minimal, readable failure examples without manual shrinker writing.
- Bug taxonomy: (1) forgot about zero, (2) cursed data types, (3) structural invariant errors. Category 3 is most relevant to our coordination work.
- Currently weak at concurrent/distributed testing (Hypothesis limitation), but that's on their roadmap.

## What's not known

- How their "Hegel skill" is structured — is it a CLAUDE.md-style instruction set? A prompt? How does it compare to our skillc-compiled skills?
- Whether PBT could be integrated into our verification pipeline (pre-commit, completion gates)
- Whether their model-based testing pattern maps onto anything in our probe/investigation framework
- Whether Antithesis's deterministic simulation approach has relevance for testing multi-agent coordination

## Constraints

- .kb/models/harness-engineering/ — our existing model of what works/doesn't in agent verification
- .kb/threads/2026-03-22-constraint-first-design-orchestration-wrong.md — constraint-first thinking
- Decision: accretion gates are advisory not blocking (2026-03-17) — relevant to "gates fail" finding

title: Architect: centralized agent lifecycle state machine in pkg/agent/lifecycle.go
description: Agent lifecycle cleanup has 4 independent gaps discovered in one session: (1) orch abandon doesn't remove orch:agent label or assignee → ghost agents in dashboard, (2) orch clean doesn't check process liveness → kills active agents, (3) daemon status reads stale files → false running/paused indicators, (4) daemon sets in_progress but has no orphan recovery → issues stuck forever. Root cause: no single authority owns lifecycle transitions. Design a centralized lifecycle package (pkg/agent/lifecycle.go) that owns every state transition. All commands (spawn, complete, abandon, clean, status) route through it. State machine: spawn→active→completing→closed, spawn→active→abandoned→open. Each transition function handles ALL steps (beads status, labels, assignee, tmux window, workspace, OpenCode session, events). Clean becomes a GC for leaked resources only. Related issues: orch-go-15a2 (clean liveness), orch-go-4omh (stale daemon status), orch-go-8tpn (orphan recovery).

Prior attempts (Feb 28 spiral) used npx playwright (built-in CLI) not the standalone package, and never completed a clean eval due to orchestrator spiral on experimental design. This time: just install it, use it, report back. Experiential eval, not a controlled A/B test.

Fix hook cwd resolution when subdirectory contains CLAUDE.md
description: Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files shift hook working directory resolution, blocking workers from running bash. Two agents hit this independently (la9ej on openscad measurement report, 8853k on e2e integration). Repro: any project with .harness/openscad/CLAUDE.md — hooks resolve cwd to the subdirectory instead of project root. The gate-orchestrator-bash-write.py and gate-git-add-all.py both affected. Root cause likely in how Claude Code resolves CLAUDE.md → project root when multiple CLAUDE.md files exist in the tree.

CROSS_REPO_ISSUE:
  repo: ~/Documents/personal/harness
  title: "harness init should not create .claude/ in subdirectories of existing git repos"
  type: bug
  priority: 2
  description: "When harness init runs inside a subdirectory of an existing git repo (e.g. .harness/openscad/), it creates a .claude/hooks/ directory there. This makes Claude Code treat the subdirectory as a separate project root, breaking hook cwd resolution for all agents working in that directory. harness init should detect existing git repos and either refuse or place hooks at the git root."

Create verification checkpoint file (~/.orch/verification-checkpoints.jsonl)
description:
Phase 1 of verifiability-first mechanical enforcement. The checkpoint file tracks which deliverables have been human-verified through both gates.

Schema per line: {beads_id, deliverable, gate1_complete (comprehension), gate2_complete (behavioral), timestamp, explain_text}

Integration points:
1. orch complete --explain 'text' writes checkpoint entry with gate1_complete=true
2. orch complete reads checkpoints — missing checkpoint for Tier 1 work = cannot close
3. Behavioral gate (gate2) tracked separately — feature/bug requires both, investigation requires only gate1

Currently: ~/.orch/verification-checkpoints.jsonl does not exist (ls returns 'No such file or directory').

The --explain flag already exists on orch complete (added by orch-go-w50) but does NOT write to a checkpoint file. It only stores as beads comment. Need the persistent checkpoint for daemon to read.

Related: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md (Phase 1: Checkpoint Infrastructure)

title: Architect owns decomposition: create plan + beads issues + integration issue for multi-component designs
description:
## Problem

Three-way ambiguity in who creates implementation issues from architect output (61msu). Architect, orchestrator, or plan creation — nobody owns it clearly. Results in component-scoped issues without integration coverage.

## Design

1. Architect Phase 5 gets new step: if recommendations involve 3+ components or multiple phases, architect creates plan artifact (`orch plan create`) AND beads issues for each component AND an integration verification issue that depends on all components.

2. Single-component architect work skips plans — creates implementation issue directly.

3. Integration issue is scoped as behavior verification ("all competitor HTTP goes through proxy"), not component verification ("middleware class exists").

4. Orchestrator Architect Handoff gate tightens: verify plan exists (if multi-component), issues exist, integration coverage present.

## Changes needed

- Architect SKILL.md Phase 5: add 5e (decomposition + plan creation + integration issue)
- Orchestrator SKILL.md Architect Handoff gate: verify integration coverage
- Plan template: add integration issue as standard element

## Evidence

pw-9knh: 6 agents completed, zero integration. All building blocks present, nothing wired together. See orch-go-61msu.

Daemon verification pause counts discovered work as unverified completions
description: The daemon verification pause threshold treats all triage:review issues equally. But there are two categories: (1) completed agent work awaiting verification, and (2) discovered work awaiting orchestrator review before spawning. Category 2 should not count toward the pause threshold. IMPORTANT: The fix is in the daemon pause counting logic, NOT in the label. triage:review on discovered work is correct — the orchestrator must review discovered work before it gets spawned. The prior attempt (120910fb0, reverted) wrongly changed the label to triage:ready, bypassing orchestrator review. The correct fix: distinguish completed-agent-work from discovered-work when counting toward the verification pause threshold.

title: Hotspot acceleration detector triggers on birth churn — false positives on new file creation
description:
HotspotAccelerationDetector measures lines-added-in-30-days but doesn't distinguish birth from accretion. This session saw 10+ false positive investigations spawned for files that were simply created recently (1 commit, all lines are 'growth'). 

Three failure modes:
1. New file creation — entire file size registers as growth (thread_test.go +570, plist_test.go +450)
2. Delete/recreate churn — net zero but shows as growth (control_cmd.go +292)
3. No path exclusions — walks into experiments/, test artifacts (orch-go-8hgtg filed separately for experiments/ exclusion)

Fix: detector should compare current size against first-seen size (or earliest commit in window), not sum of additions. A file that didn't exist 30 days ago and is now 500 lines isn't accelerating — it was born. Only flag files that existed before the window AND grew significantly within it.
