title: Completion flow: add explain-back verification step to orch complete
description: Current orch complete checks agent self-reported success (Phase:Complete, tests pass, deliverables exist). Missing: human comprehension verification. New step in orch complete: after showing synthesis/results, system asks Dylan to explain what was built and why in his own words. His explanation is captured as a beads comment or artifact. This creates: (1) An unfakeable verification gate - cant rubber-stamp a conversational explanation. (2) A comprehension record for future sessions - next Claude can read Dylans understanding. (3) The distinction between agent-done and human-verified. Design: Could be conversational (orchestrator asks, Dylan answers) or structured (fill in: what it does, why it matters, how you verified). The explain-back IS the gate - completion doesnt close until its provided. Analogy: pilot callouts in instrument flying - forces processing, not just glancing.
notes:
DESIGN SESSION OUTCOME (Feb 14):

Two-gate verification model:
1. COMPREHENSION GATE (what/so-what):
   - Orchestrator presents 'the what' - factual scaffolding of what was built
   - Dylan explains 'the so-what' - what it means for his workflow, what worries him
   - Design principles: safe to be wrong, not starting from nothing, not given the answer
   - The split: AI provides the what, human provides the so-what

2. BEHAVIORAL GATE (tiered by deliverable type):
   - Feature: run the command, see the result
   - Bug fix: reproduce the bug, confirm it is gone
   - Refactor: run tests, basic smoke test
   - Investigation/decision: no behavioral gate needed - artifact IS the deliverable

Order: comprehension first, then behavioral. No point watching something work if you dont understand what its supposed to do.

Key design constraint from Dylan: system cant do the thinking for him. Leading questions that Dylan just confirms = rubber-stamping with extra steps. The so-what must be genuinely Dylans articulation.

Tested live in session: used orch-go-66j (skill-class awareness gate fix) as test case. Dylan confirmed the interaction felt right - made him think but not too much.

title: Investigate Context Hub's annotation loop pattern — agent discovers gap, annotates, next session benefits. Compare with orch-go's kb quick pattern. Does this pattern generalize? Is there something we're missing?
description:
Context Hub (github.com/andrewyng/context-hub, 10k stars, Andrew Ng) has an annotation system where agents annotate documentation gaps during work, and those annotations persist to future sessions. Commands: chub annotate <id> <note>, chub annotate --list, chub feedback <id> up|down.

orch-go has a similar pattern via kb quick (decide/tried/constrain/question) but the two differ in interesting ways:
- Context Hub annotations are scoped to specific docs and auto-surface when those docs are fetched again
- kb quick entries are global and require manual promotion/pruning (50 active entries needed triage this session)
- Context Hub has a feedback loop to doc authors (chub feedback up/down)

Questions to explore:
1. How does Context Hub's annotation storage and retrieval work? (read the source)
2. What's the auto-surfacing mechanism? (scoped to doc ID vs global search)
3. Does orch-go's kb quick pattern have an auto-surfacing gap that this pattern could fill?
4. Is there a generalizable pattern here: scoped annotations that auto-surface at point-of-use vs global knowledge that requires search?
5. Could spawn context injection benefit from scoped annotations (e.g., per-file, per-package, per-skill annotations that auto-inject when relevant)?

title: Design: system-prompt skill injection for spawn
description:
DISCOVERY: Claude CLI supports --system-prompt and --append-system-prompt in interactive mode (not just --print). Currently skills load via ~/.claude/skills/ auto-discovery → injected as user-level context, competing with SPAWN_CONTEXT.md, CLAUDE.md, and everything else for attention. The behavioral grammars model (Feb 24 probe) found system prompt has structural priority over user-level content. The dilution curve (~4 behavioral constraint budget) was measured with skill content at user level — the effective budget at system level could be significantly higher.

OPPORTUNITY: orch spawn could pass --append-system-prompt {skill_content} to claude CLI, injecting skills at system prompt level instead of user-level auto-discovery. This would give skill content structural priority in the attention hierarchy.

QUESTIONS FOR ARCHITECT:
1. --append-system-prompt vs --system-prompt? Append preserves Claude Code base instructions. Replace gives full control but loses base behavior.
2. Should SPAWN_CONTEXT.md remain user-level while skill content moves to system level? Or should both move?
3. Does --disable-slash-commands become the default for spawns (prevent double-loading from both system-prompt injection and auto-discovery)?
4. What happens to the skillc test harness? It already uses --system-prompt — does this make test conditions match production conditions (good) or lose the ability to test user-level vs system-level injection (tradeoff)?
5. Does this change the dilution curve? The ~4 behavioral constraint budget was measured at user level. An architect should design an experiment to measure the budget at system level.

EVIDENCE:
- claude --help shows --system-prompt and --append-system-prompt without '(only works with --print)' restriction
- skillc test already uses --system-prompt successfully in --print mode
- Behavioral grammars model: .kb/global/models/behavioral-grammars/model.md (Claims 1-3 on dilution and attention)
- Feb 24 probe: system prompt has structural priority over user-level content
- Dilution threshold probe: ~4 behavioral constraint budget at user level
- Spawn code: cmd/orch/spawn_cmd.go, pkg/spawn/ — current skill injection path

Possible causes: dilution (too many competing constraints), structural insufficiency (wrong form), or inherent unenforceability. Suggested experiment: isolate constraint X with 3-form diversity and re-test.' This is the trigger mechanism for autonomous research — anomaly detection → hypothesis generation without human intervention.

title: Design evidence-tier annotation standard for KB model claims
description:
## Context

Epistemic audit (2026-03-22) found that the coordination model and other KB models suffer from **compression before validation**: local experimental results get packaged as general claims without the evidence base to support them. This is a recurring tendency, not a one-time editorial problem — the agent pipeline structurally produces it because each synthesis layer adds generality without scope-restricting.

Audit: `.kb/investigations/2026-03-22-inv-audit-coordination-model-epistemic-legitimacy.md`

## The Problem

No structural mechanism prevents a claim written as `tier: observed` from being restated elsewhere as a general principle. The tendency recurs because:
- Agents investigate, find something real, write it up at the scope of their understanding
- The orchestrator synthesizes across findings and compresses further
- Nobody in the chain is incentivized to scope-restrict

## What to Design

1. **Evidence-tier annotation standard** for claims in KB model files. Minimum viable: a per-claim field (e.g., `observed`, `working-hypothesis`, `replicated`, `validated`) that must be present when a claim is written.

2. **Provenance integration for drift detection.** The repo already has `pkg/kbmetrics/provenance.go` auditing evidence quality. Design how it detects when claim prose exceeds the declared evidence tier (e.g., a claim marked `observed` that uses language like "fundamentally flawed" or "universally").

3. **Model rewrite guidelines.** What scoped language looks like vs overclaimed language. The audit's "strongest defensible form" examples are a starting point.

## Constraints

- Must be lightweight enough that agents actually use it (one field, not a pipeline)
- Must integrate with existing provenance machinery, not create new infrastructure
- The unit of rigor is the individual claim, not the document
- Should make compression visible and auditable, not prevent it entirely

## Key Reference

The audit's recommended defensible claim form:
> "In the tested same-file parallel-edit scenarios, messaging and context-sharing did not reduce merge conflicts, while explicit structural placement eliminated them."

vs the overclaimed form:
> "Communication does not produce coordination" / "frameworks relying on messaging are fundamentally flawed"

- Nobody in the chain is incentivized to scope-restrict

## What to Design

1. **Evidence-tier annotation standard** for claims in KB model files. Minimum viable: a per-claim field (e.g., `observed`, `working-hypothesis`, `replicated`, `validated`) that must be present when a claim is written.

2. **Provenance integration for drift detection.** The repo already has `pkg/kbmetrics/provenance.go` auditing evidence quality. Design how it detects when claim prose exceeds the declared evidence tier (e.g., a claim marked `observed` that uses language like "fundamentally flawed" or "universally").

3. **Model rewrite guidelines.** What scoped language looks like vs overclaimed language. The audit's "strongest defensible form" examples are a starting point.

## Constraints

- Must be lightweight enough that agents actually use it (one field, not a pipeline)
- Must integrate with existing provenance machinery, not create new infrastructure
- The unit of rigor is the individual claim, not the document
- Should make compression visible and auditable, not prevent it entirely

## Key Reference

The audit's recommended defensible claim form:
> "In the tested same-file parallel-edit scenarios, messaging and context-sharing did not reduce merge conflicts, while explicit structural placement eliminated them."

vs the overclaimed form:
> "Communication does not produce coordination" / "frameworks relying on messaging are fundamentally flawed"

Review queue visibility: dashboard + orchestrator awareness
description:
Two visibility gaps discovered during verifiability-first bootstrap:

1. DASHBOARD: No review queue view. The dashboard shows issues by status (triage, in_progress) but has no first-class view of 'completed agents waiting for two-gate review.' Dylan had no visibility into the fact that 4 completions were waiting — had to run orch review in CLI. The review queue is the primary interface for verifiability-first: it's where Dylan does Gate 1 (comprehension) and Gate 2 (behavioral). It should be the most prominent thing in the dashboard, not invisible.

2. ORCHESTRATOR AWARENESS: The AI orchestrator should have the review queue as persistent context — like the HUD concept in orch-go-s0eg but specifically for 'N completions awaiting your review.' The orchestrator and Dylan work through the queue together (orchestrator presents the-what, Dylan explains the-so-what). The orchestrator needs to know the queue state without running orch review each time.

The orch tree CLI already has work view infrastructure (orch tree --work). The dashboard has a Knowledge Tree with work view. The data exists — daemon:ready-review label marks completed work. What's missing is surfacing it as a primary workflow, not buried in a tree of 21 issues.

Prior art: orch-go-6rw (daemon rework to ready-for-review model), orch-go-s0eg (HUD concept), orch tree CLI (work view with status).

Success criteria: Dylan opens dashboard and immediately sees 'You have N completions to review' with enough context to start Gate 1 without running CLI commands.

title: Architect: model-driven strategy — claims.yaml schema, orient consumption, daemon probe generation, completion pipeline claim updates
description:
Design the claims-as-atoms infrastructure for model-driven strategy. Thread: .kb/threads/2026-03-19-model-driven-strategy-models-generate.md

Three structural elements:
1. claims.yaml per model dir — schema: id, assertion, status (confirmed/unconfirmed/contested/stale), impact scope, evidence refs, tensions (cross-model), last_validated
2. Orient consumption — surface tensions, stale claims in active models, unconfirmed claims. Show edges not dumps.
3. Daemon probe generation — demand-driven: stale claim + model active in recent spawns = auto-probe issue. Dormant models don't trigger.
4. Completion pipeline — every finding confirms/extends/contradicts existing claims. Claim lifecycle connects investigations to claim graph.

Bootstrap with 4 models: architectural-enforcement, measurement-honesty, agent-trust-enforcement, skill-content-transfer.

Key constraint: models drive inquiry not implementation. Flow: claims → probes → evidence → strategy. Self-referential validation risk (measurement-honesty invariant 5) must be addressed in design.

Experiment C: Anticipatory placement — can static analysis predict the right attractors without failure data?
description:
## Context

If an LLM can read a codebase and two task descriptions and predict non-overlapping insertion points WITHOUT seeing any trial failures, then Layer 1 (structural scaffolding) solves most of the coordination problem without needing the failure→attractor loop at all.

## Design

Give claude-haiku-4-5 (same model as agents):
- The target codebase (display.go, display_test.go)
- Both task descriptions (Agent A: FormatBytes, Agent B: FormatRate)
- Instruction: "Generate non-overlapping insertion points for both tasks"

Run 20 trials with the LLM-generated placements (10 simple, 10 complex).

**Control:** Compare against human-generated placement (existing 20/20 success condition).

**Measure:** Success rate. Are LLM-predicted attractors as effective as human-designed ones?

## Hypothesis

High confidence this works for simple cases — the LLM can read the code, see the natural insertion points, and assign different ones. Complex cases (VisualWidth + FormatTable modifying multiple files) are the real test. If it works: automated attractor scaffolding is viable as a coordination primitive.

## Significance

This is the most consequential experiment in the chain. If anticipatory placement works, the coordination problem reduces to: "have an orchestrator read the code before agents run." That's automatable today. If it fails, you need the failure→attractor loop (Experiment A), which is slower and more complex.

## Key files

- experiments/coordination-demo/redesign/ (existing infrastructure)
- .kb/models/coordination/model.md (framework being tested)

Experiment B: Attractor decay — how fast does coordination degrade when attractors go stale?
description:
## Context

The coordination model shows attractors produce 100% success. The governance self-accretion probe showed learned attractors can go stale (launchd post-mortem: 186 investigations reinforced wrong premise). Open question: how fast does attractor-based coordination degrade when the codebase changes underneath the attractors?

## Design

Start with correct placement attractors (100% success baseline). Then change the codebase:
- Phase 1: Rename target functions (FormatDurationShort → FormatElapsed)
- Phase 2: Reorganize file structure (move functions between files)
- Phase 3: Add new functions that create alternative "semantically correct" insertion points

Run 10 trials after each phase with the ORIGINAL (now stale) attractors.

**Measure:** Success rate degradation curve. How many codebase changes before attractors become ineffective? Is degradation gradual or cliff-edge?

## Hypothesis

Phase 1 (rename) will cause immediate failure — placement says "after FormatDurationShort" but the function doesn't exist. Phase 2 (reorganize) may partially work if file references still resolve. Phase 3 (new alternatives) is the interesting case — attractors may still technically work but point to suboptimal locations.

This tests the SENSOR requirement: how fast must you detect attractor decay to maintain coordination?

## Key files

- experiments/coordination-demo/redesign/ (existing infrastructure)
- .kb/models/coordination/model.md (framework being tested)

Investigate: revisit OpenCode model routing for GPT 5.4 — multi-model spawn, stall rate testing, cost comparison
description:
## Thread

OpenCode (Dylan's fork of sst/opencode) currently routes all agent work through Claude Code sessions using Anthropic's Claude Max subscription. Anthropic prohibits Max usage outside Claude Code. OpenAI allows ChatGPT Pro subscription access via 3rd party OAuth. GPT 5.4 now outperforms Opus 4.6.

The current architecture: orch-go daemon → OpenCode API → Claude Code → Claude Max. This creates total Anthropic lock-in at the execution layer.

## What to investigate

1. **OpenCode model routing:** Can OpenCode route to OpenAI models (GPT 5.4) in addition to/instead of Claude? What would need to change in the fork? Check opencode's model provider abstraction — it may already support multiple providers since it's an sst project.

2. **orch-go spawn model selection:** How hard is it to route specific skills to specific models? The daemon already has model inference from skill metadata. Could high-protocol skills (architect, investigation) stay on Claude while simpler skills (feature-impl, systematic-debugging) use GPT 5.4?

3. **GPT 5.4 stall rate:** The CLAUDE.md gotcha says "Non-Anthropic models: GPT-4o/GPT-5.2-codex have 67-87% stall rates on protocol-heavy skills." GPT 5.4 is a generation ahead. Design a quick test: spawn 5 feature-impl tasks on GPT 5.4 via OpenCode, measure completion rate. Compare to the historical Anthropic baseline.

4. **OAuth access pattern:** If OpenCode can consume OpenAI via OAuth (ChatGPT Pro subscription), what does the auth flow look like? Is it as simple as adding an OpenAI provider config, or does it need protocol changes?

5. **Cost comparison:** Claude Max ($200/mo unlimited via Claude Code) vs ChatGPT Pro (pricing?) via OpenCode. What's the break-even if GPT 5.4 is per-token through API vs subscription through OAuth?

## Constraints
- OpenCode fork: ~/Documents/personal/opencode
- orch-go model routing: pkg/spawn/config.go, pkg/daemon/daemon.go
- Current model gotcha: CLAUDE.md "Gotchas" section
- accounts.yaml: ~/.orch/accounts.yaml (multi-account routing)

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
FRAME: ## Thread

OpenCode (Dylan's fork of sst/opencode) currently routes all agent work through Claude Code sessions using Anthropic's Claude Max subscription. Anthropic prohibits Max usage outside Claude Code. OpenAI allows ChatGPT Pro subscription access via 3rd party OAuth. GPT 5.4 now outperforms Opus 4.6.

The current architecture: orch-go daemon → OpenCode API → Claude Code → Claude Max. This creates total Anthropic lock-in at the execution layer.

## What to investigate

1. **OpenCode model routing:** Can OpenCode route to OpenAI models (GPT 5.4) in addition to/instead of Claude? What would need to change in the fork? Check opencode's model provider abstraction — it may already support multiple providers since it's an sst project.

2. **orch-go spawn model selection:** How hard is it to route specific skills to specific models? The daemon already has model inference from skill metadata. Could high-protocol skills (architect, investigation) stay on Claude while simpler skills (feature-impl, systematic-debugging) use GPT 5.4?

3. **GPT 5.4 stall rate:** The CLAUDE.md gotcha says "Non-Anthropic models: GPT-4o/GPT-5.2-codex have 67-87% stall rates on protocol-heavy skills." GPT 5.4 is a generation ahead. Design a quick test: spawn 5 feature-impl tasks on GPT 5.4 via OpenCode, measure completion rate. Compare to the historical Anthropic baseline.

4. **OAuth access pattern:** If OpenCode can consume OpenAI via OAuth (ChatGPT Pro subscription), what does the auth flow look like? Is it as simple as adding an OpenAI provider config, or does it need protocol changes?

5. **Cost comparison:** Claude Max ($200/mo unlimited via Claude Code) vs ChatGPT Pro (pricing?) via OpenCode. What's the break-even if GPT 5.4 is per-token through API vs subscription through OAuth?

## Constraints
- OpenCode fork: ~/Documents/personal/opencode
- orch-go model routing: pkg/spawn/config.go, pkg/daemon/daemon.go
- Current model gotcha: CLAUDE.md "Gotchas" section

title: Add 'orch account configure' command for setting tier/role/config_dir
description:
## Problem
The only way to set account metadata (tier, role, config_dir) is manual YAML editing. This is fragile and the metadata gets lost (see: recurring metadata loss bug).

## Solution
Add `orch account configure <name>` command:
```
orch account configure work --tier 20x --role spillover --config-dir ~/.claude
orch account configure personal --role primary
```

Does a targeted load-modify-save that only updates specified fields.

## Acceptance
- `orch account configure <name> --tier/--role/--config-dir` works
- Only specified fields are modified
- `orch account list` shows updated values

title: orch stats 7-day window underreports burst-usage skills like codebase-audit
Design session: structural solutions to accretion gravity in agentic coding
description:
## Problem (Systemic)

Accretion gravity is a fundamental constraint of task-scoped agents: agents add code where similar code exists because that's their only signal about where code belongs. No agent will spontaneously create a new package — that's not in their task.

The prior architect session (orch-go-4mu) designed 4 detection/warning layers (CLAUDE.md, spawn gates, completion gates, coaching). But detection without direction creates stuck agents. The principle says "structural constraints, not better agents" — and all 4 layers try to make better-informed agents.

## The Gap

None of the proposed solutions tell agents WHERE to put code. They only say "don't add here." An agent told "don't add to spawn_cmd.go" still doesn't know that pkg/spawn/gates/hotspot.go is the right target.

## Questions to Explore

1. **Why do agents accrete?** Because file proximity is their only architectural signal. What other signals could exist?
2. **What would a structural fix look like?** Not detection — actual constraints that make accretion physically harder than the alternative.
3. **Is extraction-first viable?** Before adding features to bloated files, could we spawn extraction first to create landing zones?
4. **Can spawn context carry architectural intent?** Orchestrator decides "this code goes in pkg/X/Y.go" at spawn time — agent doesn't choose.
5. **What do other agentic coding systems do?** Is this a solved problem anywhere?
6. **Does package structure itself prevent accretion?** If pkg/spawn/gates/ exists with clear purpose, do agents naturally use it?

## What This Is NOT

- NOT another detection/warning system
- NOT CLAUDE.md rules agents will ignore under pressure
- NOT gates that fire after the damage is done

## Constraint

This is a design session for Dylan + orchestrator, not a spawnable investigation. The solution needs to be general enough to apply to any agentic coding system, not just orch-go.
notes:
## Design Session Outcome (Feb 14 2026)

Rejected architect's 4-layer detection/warning approach. Detection without direction creates stuck agents.

**Actual solution: Invert the gravity field.**

1. Extract concerns from monolithic files into focused packages (strangler fig)
2. Agents naturally discover and route to focused packages via search (proven by probe orch-go-8ao)
3. Monolithic files shrink as new work routes to attractors AND agents pull existing code into them

**Probe evidence:** Agent given "add rate-limit gate to spawn" with NO mention of pkg/spawn/gates/ — created ratelimit.go there anyway AND extracted 173 lines from spawn_cmd.go.

**Who extracts:** Two tracks:
1. Orchestrator judgment at triage time (manual, current)
2. Daemon auto-extraction when targeting CRITICAL hotspots (automated, orch-go-TBD)

**Key insight:** Agents don't need architectural docs or rules. They need directory structure that makes the right choice obvious from search results. The file system IS the architecture.

Investigation: false coherence in the orchestration system — where does the system produce confidence that doesn't correspond to reality?

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
Phase: Decompose — Breaking false coherence question into 4 parallel subproblems:

1. VERIFICATION THEATER — Where do tests/gates/checks create appearance of quality without ensuring it? Gap between unit test coverage and behavioral verification. Focus: trigger detector tests, gate tests, completion verification pipeline.

2. METRIC CAMOUFLAGE — Where do healthy-looking numbers hide problems? Which metrics look identical whether system works or spins wheels? Focus: completion counts, throughput, gate fire rates, orient measurements.

3. AUTONOMOUS CONFIDENCE — Where does daemon produce confident outputs without adequate grounding? Focus: trigger system, skill inference, spawn decisions, issue creation, budget mechanisms.

4. KB COHERENCE SIGNALS — Where do KB artifacts create appearance of understanding without verified knowledge? Focus: 91.6% orphan rate, investigation quality, decision staleness, model probe coverage.

Each subproblem is independently answerable and together they cover the full question.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Exploration synthesis: 8-pattern false coherence taxonomy across 4 tiers, ranked by harm. Root cause: self-referential measurement (system grades own homework). Workers: 4 spawned, 4 completed, 1 judge completed. Contested findings: 3 (orphan rate dual nature, advisory gates policy/measurement mismatch, mock-boundary vs detector design).

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
EXPLAIN-BACK:
Exploration decomposed false coherence into 4 parallel investigations. Produced 8-pattern taxonomy. Root cause: self-referential measurement — system evaluates itself using only its own output stream. Created 6 follow-up issues across 3 tracks (quick wins, architectural, pre-enable).

title: Design artifact sync mechanism — skills, CLAUDE.md, and models drift as features land, soft conventions haven't held
description:
Recurring coordination failure: agents ship features but don't update the artifacts that reference those features (orchestrator skill, CLAUDE.md, harness-engineering model, etc). Dylan flags this periodically. Prior soft fixes (reminders in skills, completion checklist items) haven't stuck — consistent with harness model prediction that convention without gate erodes.

Two design directions to evaluate:
1. Completion gate: detect that agent modified files covered by a skill/model, block until referencing artifact is updated. Risk: high FP rate, most feature-impl agents shouldn't edit skills.
2. Periodic sync agent: OpenAI's 'garbage collection' pattern — background agent reviews recent commits against artifact inventory, produces update PRs. Decouples feature work from artifact maintenance.

Key question: is the update responsibility per-agent (gate) or periodic (sync agent)? Per-agent is harder but immediate. Periodic is easier but introduces lag.

Evidence: this session alone shipped --explore flag, orch review synthesize, exploration-judge skill, gate signal-vs-noise fixes — none updated the orchestrator skill or CLAUDE.md. The drift is real-time.

title: Rework explain-back gate: conversational via orchestrator, not CLI prompts
description:
The current explain-back implementation (pkg/verify/explain_back.go) uses interactive CLI prompts - three fixed questions that Dylan types answers to in a terminal. This doesnt match how Dylan actually works: he works through the AI orchestrator conversationally.

CURRENT (wrong model):
  Dylan runs orch complete → CLI prompts 'What did you build?' → Dylan types answer → repeat x3

CORRECT (matches actual workflow):
  Orchestrator presents 'the what' → Dylan explains 'the so-what' conversationally → orchestrator captures explanation → orchestrator runs orch complete --explain 'summary'

CHANGES NEEDED:
1. Remove interactive PromptExplainBack() stdin/stdout prompting from pkg/verify/explain_back.go
2. Change --skip-explain-back flag to --explain 'text' flag on orch complete
3. Gate on --explain being non-empty when explain-back gate is active
4. Store the explanation as beads comment (this part already works)
5. Keep GateExplainBack constant and skip logic for orchestrator sessions / untracked agents

The conversational quality check (is Dylans explanation sufficient?) stays with the AI orchestrator, not the CLI. The CLIs job is: accept explanation, store it, gate on non-empty.

WHY: Fixed CLI questions can be answered mechanically (rubber-stamping). Conversational verification cant - the orchestrator can follow up, challenge, ask 'what worries you about it?' This was validated in the design session where we verified 4 deliverables conversationally and it worked well.

CONSTRAINT: AI sycophancy undermines explain-back verification (kb-afa611). The orchestrator must not mirror Dylans answers back or validate incomplete understanding.

Prior commit: 61ee67c3 (feat: add explain-back verification gate to orch complete)
Files to modify: pkg/verify/explain_back.go, cmd/orch/complete_cmd.go

title: orch spawn should explicitly set CLAUDE_CONTEXT=worker for spawned agents
description: Hardening: orch spawn should explicitly pass CLAUDE_CONTEXT=worker when launching agents via Claude CLI. Currently workers inherit whatever CLAUDE_CONTEXT is in the environment, which caused contamination when CLAUDE_CONTEXT=orchestrator was set globally in settings.json. Even though that was reverted, explicit is better than implicit — workers should always declare their context so hooks (gate-orchestrator-code-access.py, gate-orchestrator-task-tool.py) reliably skip them. Implementation: in pkg/spawn/claude.go, add CLAUDE_CONTEXT=worker to the env vars passed to the claude CLI invocation.

title: Extract periodic task scheduler from runDaemonLoop (cmd/orch/daemon.go)
orch serve burns 170-270% CPU immediately after restart — likely hot loop in status/workspace scanning
description:
## Symptom
Fresh `orch serve --port 3348` hits 170% CPU within seconds, climbs to 267%+ sustained. Consumes ~3 cores continuously.

## What's known
- Pegs CPU on fresh restart — not a memory leak over time, it's hot from the start
- Thread '.kb/threads/2026-03-20-silent-cost-accumulation' identified that `getCompletionsForReview` scans 1,106 workspaces across 19 projects on every status query
- Dashboard polls multiple endpoints on 60s intervals (agents SSE, beads, harness, verification)
- The serve binary is built from stale code (auto-rebuild was failing due to cleanup agent build breaks)

## What's not known
- Which endpoint/handler is the hot path (profile needed)
- Whether SSE agent polling or periodic workspace scanning is the primary cost
- Whether the workspace scan got worse after workspace count grew

## Constraints
- No local agent state rule means we can't cache — but we can reduce scan frequency or scope
- Dashboard is Dylan's only observability layer — can't degrade it

## Repro
```bash
orch-dashboard restart
ps aux -r | head -5  # observe orch serve at 170%+ immediately
```

title: Inventory current system into core/substrate/adjacent
description:
Question:
Given the newly accepted product boundary, which current systems in orch-go are core, which are substrate, and which are adjacent assets that should not keep bending the main product?

What's known:
- The strongest differentiated layer is thread/comprehension/coordination
- The repo still presents itself and behaves in many places like an orchestration/execution system first
- There is significant surface area in spawn, daemon, verify, dashboard, and backend integration code

What's not known:
- Which major subsystems should remain first-class product investments
- Which systems should be maintained as substrate only
- Which efforts should be extracted, de-emphasized, or treated as adjacent assets

Constraints:
- Use the boundary from .kb/decisions/2026-03-26-thread-comprehension-layer-is-primary-product.md
- Use the sequencing logic from .kb/plans/2026-03-26-thread-comprehension-consolidation.md Phase 2
- Produce a decision-oriented inventory, not a generic code tour
- Bias toward helping future prioritization and deletion decisions

Deliverable:
A written subsystem map classifying the major product areas into core / substrate / adjacent, with rationale and the most important implications for future investment.

title: Spawn context kb relevance: query matches skill names instead of domain topics, drowning task-specific FRAME
description:
PROBLEM: orch spawn assembles spawn context by running kb context with the task title as query. For 'Architect: Redesign pricing comparison KPIs', the query matched on 'architect' and 'redesign' as keywords, pulling 22 entries (66KB) of orch-go infrastructure knowledge (gate calibration, toolchain failures, coaching plugin probes). Zero entries about toolshed pricing or KPI design.

TWO ROOT CAUSES:
1. Query relevance — kb context matches on skill/concept names (architect, redesign) rather than domain topics (pricing, KPI). Global/orch-go entries dominate over project-specific entries.
2. FRAME not in spawn context — the beads issue FRAME comment (containing the actual strategic insight) is not assembled into SPAWN_CONTEXT.md. Agent must manually bd show to find it.

EXPECTED: Spawn context should (1) include FRAME from beads comments front and center, (2) prefer project-specific kb entries over global ones, (3) filter by domain relevance not keyword overlap with skill names.

title: Fix sketchybar orch widget popup not opening on click — click handler on item but popup owned by bracket
description: Popup does not open when clicking the orch widget in sketchybar. The click subscriber is on the orch item (line 272) but the popup is owned by orch_bracket. Sketchybar routes clicks to the item, not the bracket. Fix: subscribe mouse.clicked on the bracket as well, or move click handling to the bracket. File: ~/.config/sketchybar/items/widgets/orch.lua. Event provider and bar display work correctly (verified via debug log).

Sibling agents in tmux die when orch complete runs for another agent
description: Problem: When orch complete runs for agent A, sibling agents B and C (running in the same workers-orch-go tmux session) die. Their tmux windows disappear and they show as phantom in orch status. This has happened repeatedly this session — every time we complete an agent, other in-flight agents die. The t95 fix (overmind restart api instead of kill) landed but agents still die. Evidence from this session: (1) orch-go-9 was Phase: Implementing when orch-go-8 completion killed it. (2) orch-go-7 was Phase: Implementing when orch-go-8 completion killed it. (3) Earlier: pw-8966 and orch-go-1 died the same way. Timeline: agents spawned ~23:30, orch-go-8 completed at ~23:56, orch-go-9 and orch-go-7 went phantom immediately after. Possible causes: (1) make install replaces the orch binary mid-execution for active agents. (2) overmind restart api has side effects on tmux. (3) Claude CLI sessions crash when OpenCode server restarts. (4) Some other orch complete side effect kills sibling windows.

Add --related flag to kb quick commands for tree lineage
description:
Add --related <path> flag to kb quick decide/constrain/tried so quick entries attach to parent artifacts in the knowledge tree.

DESIGN DOC: docs/designs/2026-02-15-collaborative-tree-building.md (Phase 5)

SCOPE:
1. Add --related <path> flag to kb quick decide, kb quick constrain, kb quick tried
2. Store relationship in JSONL entry (new field: related_artifact)
3. Update pkg/tree/ extraction to include related quick entries as children of parent artifact
4. Quick entries appear in both knowledge view (under parent) and timeline view (chronological)

EXAMPLE:
kb quick decide "serialize verification" --reason "..." --related decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md

DEPENDS ON: decision kb-bcd869

NOTE: kb quick lives in kb-cli repo (~/Documents/personal/kb-cli), not orch-go. The tree extraction (pkg/tree/) is in orch-go and would need to read the --related field from JSONL. This issue may need to be split: one for kb-cli (flag), one for orch-go (tree extraction).

VERIFICATION:
- kb quick decide "X" --related decisions/some-decision.md stores relationship
- orch tree shows quick entry as child of related artifact
- Works for decide, constrain, tried commands

title: Daemon routes architect issues to feature-impl — skill:architect label dropped or ignored in routing
description:
## Symptom

Issues labeled skill:architect are being routed to feature-impl instead of architect. Observed ~7 times by Dylan.

## Context

orch-go-z5uck (completed this session) fixed skill:* label passthrough through SpawnWork interface — the daemon was correctly inferring skill from labels but the skill parameter was dropped at the Spawner.SpawnWork boundary. That fix landed today.

If this is still happening AFTER that fix, there's a second routing failure. If all 7 observations were before the fix, this may already be resolved.

## What to check

1. Are the 7 observations all pre-fix (before commit 8248e8ed4)?
2. After the fix, does a skill:architect labeled task correctly spawn as architect?
3. Is the daemon's type-based fallback (task → feature-impl) still overriding the skill label in any code path?
4. Check pkg/daemon/skill_inference.go, pkg/daemon/compliance.go, and the SpawnWork interface for any remaining paths where skill label gets lost.

## Repro

Create a task issue with skill:architect label, release via triage:ready, observe which skill the daemon spawns.

Implement orch benchmark CLI surface and suite config loading
description:
Build the top-level  command surface and load benchmark suites from a YAML file. Include flag validation, model alias resolution, dry-run output, and clear boundaries that keep live reliability runs separate from Manage OS-level immutability for control plane files.

The control plane consists of ~/.claude/settings.json and enforcement hook
scripts (PreToolUse, Stop events). These files define agent constraints and
are protected from modification using macOS chflags uchg.

This is the "hard harness" layer — it makes the wrong path structurally
impossible rather than relying on agent instructions.

Commands:
  init     Set up governance artifacts for a project
  check    Scan project for file accretion violations
  lock     Apply chflags uchg to all control plane files
  unlock   Remove chflags uchg to allow intentional modifications
  status   Show lock state of all control plane files
  verify   Check that all control plane files are locked
  snapshot Capture directory-level line count snapshot
  report   Show harness measurement report (orch-native)

Workflow:
  orch harness unlock    # Allow modifications
  <edit settings.json or hooks>
  orch harness lock      # Re-protect

Most subcommands delegate to the standalone 'harness' binary.
Install it with: go install github.com/dylan-conlin/harness@latest

Usage:
  orch-go harness [command]

Available Commands:
  audit              Audit gate invocations, fire rates, costs, and anomalies
  check              Scan project for file accretion violations
  gate-effectiveness Analyze whether gate enforcement improves agent quality
  init               Initialize governance artifacts in the current project
  lock               Lock control plane files (chflags uchg)
  report             Show harness measurement report with falsification verdicts
  snapshot           Capture directory-level line count snapshot for accretion velocity tracking
  status             Show lock state of all control plane files
  unlock             Unlock control plane files (chflags nouchg)
  verify             Verify all control plane files are locked (for pre-commit hooks)

Flags:
  -h, --help   help for harness

Global Flags:
      --server string   OpenCode server URL (default "http://127.0.0.1:4096")

Use "orch-go harness [command] --help" for more information about a command. analytics.

title: Headless spawn doesn't set agent working directory to --workdir/cwd
description: Problem: When spawning headless agents for cross-project work (e.g., from orch-go orchestrator targeting price-watch), the agent's shell starts in orch-go (likely the daemon's cwd or a hardcoded default) instead of the target project directory. PROJECT_DIR is included in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md as prompt text, but this doesn't control the actual working directory of the spawned session. The agent has to manually cd, and bd commands fail before it does (e.g., 'bd comment pw-w7z0' fails because it's looking in orch-go's .beads/ which has no pw- prefix issues). Evidence: Agent pw-feat-build-scsoauthclient-service-13feb-7c9d started in /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/personal/orch-go instead of /Users/dylanconlin/Documents/work/SendCutSend/scs-special-projects/price-watch. SPAWN_CONTEXT.md had correct PROJECT_DIR but shell cwd was wrong. Agent had to cd manually and bd comment failed before it did. Impact: P1 because this affects ALL cross-project headless spawns. Agents waste context discovering wrong directory, bd phase reporting fails, and early commands operate on wrong project's beads DB. Fix: The headless spawn backend (OpenCode HTTP API) needs to set the session's working directory to the --workdir flag value or the orchestrator's cwd at spawn time.

comments.text: Phase: Complete - Exploration synthesis: Knowledge accretion has 4 blind spots the model missed. (1) PROMPT CONTEXT is a 5th confirmed substrate — CLAUDE.md grew 8x in 91 days, 92% passive reference, 3 contradictions, total blast radius (every agent reads it). (2) Only 4 of 31 proposed interventions (13%) demonstrably reduce accretion. Effectiveness hierarchy: structural attractors > signaling > blocking gates > advisory > metrics-only. (3) Governance infrastructure is itself accreting: 35% of codebase, 85% of daemon tasks, accelerating from 18% to 23% in March. (4) Human feedback channel is structurally broken: 0 reworks in 1,102 completions because rework has 8 friction points vs 1 for re-spawn. Workers: 4 spawned + 1 judge, all 5 completed. Contested findings: 1 (35% governance vs 13% effectiveness creates inert measurement overhead).

title: Account re-auth flow drops metadata (tier/role/config_dir)
description:
## Problem
When a token refresh fails and user follows the recommended re-auth flow (`orch account remove work && orch account add work`), all account metadata (tier, role, config_dir) is lost. AddAccount in oauth.go:311 creates a bare Account struct with only Email, RefreshToken, Source.

This is a recurring bug — metadata has been manually restored multiple times.

## Root Cause
Two issues:
1. `AddAccount()` creates a bare `Account{}` struct without merging existing metadata
2. The recommended re-auth guidance in `TokenRefreshError.ActionableGuidance()` tells users to remove+add, which destroys metadata by design

## Fix Options
**Option A (merge on add):** In `AddAccount()`, check if account exists and preserve Tier/Role/ConfigDir from existing entry before overwriting  
**Option B (preserve on remove):** Store metadata separately so it survives remove+add cycles  
**Option A is simpler and sufficient.**

Also: consider adding race protection (file locking) to SaveConfig since daemon, dashboard, and CLI all do concurrent load-modify-save cycles on accounts.yaml.

## Acceptance
- Re-auth flow preserves tier/role/config_dir
- Test: add account with metadata, remove+add, verify metadata survives
- Bonus: concurrent SaveConfig calls don't clobber each other

title: Design OpenClaw worker backend migration while keeping Claude Code as orchestrator frontend
description:
Question:
If orch-go keeps its methodology and orchestrator conversation model, what is the cleanest way to replace the OpenCode worker path with an OpenClaw-backed execution layer?

What's known:
- OpenClaw exposes a richer programmable control plane than OpenCode for session lifecycle and waiting.
- orch-go's value is the coordination/methodology layer, not owning more execution plumbing than necessary.
- The current OpenCode fork is expensive relative to its strategic value.

What the design should answer:
- Where the backend boundary should sit between orch-go and OpenClaw
- What Claude Code-specific assumptions need to be removed from worker plumbing
- How to preserve current orchestration primitives, beads lifecycle, and completion workflow
- How to keep Claude CLI fallback available during migration
- What can be deleted if OpenClaw becomes the worker substrate

Constraints:
- Preserve Claude Code as frontend/orchestrator for now
- Do not assume frontend replacement is in scope
- Favor a minimal migration path over a greenfield rewrite

Relevant prior artifacts:
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-23-inv-investigate-openclaw-current-state-platform.md
- .kb/investigations/2026-03-23-inv-investigate-openclaw-external-api-surface.md
- .kb/models/opencode-fork/probes/2026-03-24-probe-fork-necessity-assessment.md
- .kb/threads/2026-03-24-openclaw-migration-from-claude-code.md

Deliverable:
A phased migration design with seams, risks, deletion opportunities, and rollback points.
acceptance_criteria:
- Propose a phased migration path, not just an end state
- Identify which current subsystems survive unchanged vs must be replaced
- Define rollback/fallback strategy during migration
- Make clear whether OpenCode remains temporary bridge or should be removed

title: Experiment: Git-merge-aware messaging — does correcting the false merge model change coordination outcomes?
description:
## Context

The coordination model identifies a specific, falsifiable mechanism for why messaging fails: agents have a false model of git merge conflicts. They believe same-point additions with different function names merge cleanly. This false model persists even through explicit self-checking (gate condition, 20/20 CONFLICT).

The model flags this as "the cheapest untested intervention" (line 300 of model.md).

## Task

Design and run a messaging variant that educates agents about git merge mechanics.

**Design:** Same messaging condition from the 4-condition experiment, but agent prompts include explicit git merge education: "When two agents both insert code at the same position in a file, git cannot merge them even if the function names differ. You must choose physically separated insertion points — at least 3 lines apart from each other's likely insertion point."

**Key question:** Does correcting the false merge model reduce the conflict rate?

**Expected outcomes:**
- If conflict rate drops significantly → the false merge model IS the core mechanism, and Claim 1 narrows to "communication without correct merge models doesn't coordinate"
- If conflict rate stays at 100% → semantic-correctness bias dominates even when agents understand merge mechanics (stronger claim)

**Constraints:**
- Use same model (claude-haiku-4-5), same tasks, same N=10 per task type (20 total)
- Compare directly against original messaging condition (20/20 CONFLICT baseline)
- The only change should be the merge education in the prompt — isolate the variable
- Reference: experiments/coordination-demo/redesign/ for harness, model.md Claim 6 analysis

## What's known
- Messaging: 20/20 CONFLICT, 18/20 had coordination plans written
- Gate: 20/20 CONFLICT, agents performed check and reported "no conflict expected"
- Agent plans consistently chose "after FormatDurationShort" as insertion point
- False merge model identified in Claim 6 scope probe

title: Account routing uses weekly usage instead of 5-hour window, ignores tier
description: Account routing heuristic needs two fixes: (1) Route based on 5-hour window headroom, not weekly — the 5-hour window is what actually blocks agents. (2) Factor in tier when comparing headroom — 20x at 86% weekly has far more absolute remaining capacity than 5x at 57%. The routing should pick the account with the most absolute 5-hour headroom (percentage remaining * tier multiplier).

Wire VerificationTracker into daemon run loop (dead code from orch-go-7jl)
description:
VerificationTracker was implemented by orch-go-7jl (commit 85a0e021) with working tests. But it is NEVER CALLED in the daemon's actual run loop.

Evidence:
- pkg/daemon/daemon.go lines 201-204: VerificationTracker field exists on Daemon struct
- pkg/daemon/daemon.go lines 235, 262: NewVerificationTracker() called in constructors
- grep for RecordCompletion/IsPaused/RecordHumanVerification in daemon.go and completion.go: ZERO results

The tracker has 3 methods that must be wired:
1. RecordCompletion() - must be called after each daemon auto-completion
2. IsPaused() - must be checked before daemon spawns new agents
3. RecordHumanVerification() - must be called when orch complete runs (manual human verification)

Without this wiring, the daemon spawns indefinitely without human verification — the exact failure mode the verifiability-first decision was created to prevent (26-day spiral, 1163 commits, zero human involvement).

Also need: orch daemon resume command to call RecordHumanVerification when Dylan is ready to release more work.

Related: .kb/decisions/2026-02-14-verifiability-first-hard-constraint.md (Constraint 3: Mechanical Enforcement)

title: Ghost slot: orch complete doesn't kill tmux windows for child agents spawned by parent
description: When an agent (e.g., codebase-audit) spawns child agents (e.g., feature-impl), each child gets its own tmux window. orch complete on the parent kills only the parent window. The child window survives, daemon counts it as active, hits capacity ceiling with phantom agents. The 46cl fix (IsPaneActive) only catches dead panes — this is a live pane from a completed agent. Need: orch complete should discover and kill all tmux windows associated with a beads ID, not just the one in the workspace manifest.

Audit 38 stale decisions: which are still enforced in code, which are orphaned governance?
description:
## Context

kb reflect reports 38/62 decisions (61%) as stale — 0 citations, no tests/gates/hooks enforcing them. Many date from the governance-stripping era (health scores, spawn gates, self-review gates) and may have been superseded by removal.

## What's known

- Decisions live in `.kb/decisions/` and `.kb/global/decisions/`
- "Stale" = 0 citations in kb reflect output
- Many decisions from Feb-early Mar were about gates/governance that were subsequently removed (e.g., health score gates, self-review gates, blocking accretion gates)
- Decision 2026-03-17-accretion-gates-advisory-not-blocking.md explicitly superseded earlier blocking decisions

## What needs doing

For each of the 38 stale decisions:
1. Check if the decision is still enforced anywhere (code, hooks, CLAUDE.md, skill files)
2. Classify as: ACTIVE (still enforced), SUPERSEDED (replaced by later decision), REMOVED (the thing it governed no longer exists), or ASPIRATIONAL (never enforced)
3. For SUPERSEDED/REMOVED: recommend archival with reason
4. For ASPIRATIONAL: flag — these are the interesting ones (decisions we made but never acted on)

## Output

Produce a classification table in SYNTHESIS.md. Do NOT archive anything — just classify and recommend. The orchestrator will make final calls.

## Constraints

- kb decisions list: `.kb/decisions/` and `.kb/global/decisions/`
- Check enforcement via: grep in pkg/, cmd/, skills/src/, .claude/ hooks, CLAUDE.md files
- Decision 2026-03-17-accretion-gates-advisory-not-blocking.md is the key supersession decision

Design adversarial layer for knowledge pipeline — gate that prevents closed-loop theory escalation
description: The system's investigate→model→probe→confirm pipeline has no challenge step. Agents optimize for coherence, probes confirm within the framing, vocabulary inflates observations into theory. Result: published overclaimed blog post (harness engineering). Design a mechanical gate that would have caught this before publication. Post-mortem: (1) No adversarial layer — every agent builds on premises, none challenges framing. (2) Coherence optimization — probes tested 15 counterexamples, dismissed all with refinements (epicycles). (3) Vocabulary inflation — 'file growth' became 'accretion dynamics' became 'substrate-independent physics.' (4) Self-referencing evidence — models cite probes that cite the model. (5) Publication readiness treated as validation. Constraint: the fix itself must not be susceptible to the same closed-loop failure mode.

title: Probe: Validate gate/attractor mechanism distinction against external framework evidence
description:
## Context

The knowledge-accretion model probes established an effectiveness hierarchy from orch-go evidence:

Structural attractors > Signaling > Blocking gates > Advisory gates > Metrics-only

Only 4/31 interventions demonstrably reduced their targets. Every blocking gate was bypassed 100%. Attractors (model/probe directories, skill priming) cut orphan rates in half.

Hypothesis: this pattern is general, not orch-go-specific. External frameworks that work use attractors; those that fail use gates.

## Task

Re-read the external evidence already collected in .kb/investigations/2026-03-22-research-test-coordination-protocol-primitives-external-frameworks.md through the gate/attractor lens. For each framework, classify its coordination mechanisms as gate-based (blocking/checking) or attractor-based (structural destination/priming):

- CrewAI (broken Route) — gate or attractor implementation?
- LangGraph (broken Throttle) — gate or attractor?
- OpenAI Agents SDK (broken Sequence) — gate or attractor?
- Claude Agent SDK (broken Route+Align) — gate or attractor?
- Anthropic production system (works) — gate or attractor?
- autoresearch (works via N=1) — gate or attractor?

Also check: does McEntire's hierarchy→swarm→pipeline degradation track with more gate-based and less attractor-based coordination?

## Success criteria

- Each framework classified by mechanism type
- Assessment: do working systems correlate with attractors and failing systems with gates?
- If yes: the gate/attractor dimension is externally validated
- If no: the distinction is orch-go-specific — document what the external pattern actually is

## Key files

- .kb/investigations/2026-03-22-research-test-coordination-protocol-primitives-external-frameworks.md
- .kb/models/knowledge-accretion/probes/2026-03-20-probe-intervention-effectiveness-audit.md (the 31-intervention audit)
- .kb/models/knowledge-accretion/probes/2026-03-20-probe-governance-infrastructure-self-accretion.md

## Task

Re-read the external evidence already collected in .kb/investigations/2026-03-22-research-test-coordination-protocol-primitives-external-frameworks.md through the gate/attractor lens. For each framework, classify its coordination mechanisms as gate-based (blocking/checking) or attractor-based (structural destination/priming):

- CrewAI (broken Route) — gate or attractor implementation?
- LangGraph (broken Throttle) — gate or attractor?
- OpenAI Agents SDK (broken Sequence) — gate or attractor?
- Claude Agent SDK (broken Route+Align) — gate or attractor?
- Anthropic production system (works) — gate or attractor?
- autoresearch (works via N=1) — gate or attractor?

Also check: does McEntire's hierarchy→swarm→pipeline degradation track with more gate-based and less attractor-based coordination?

## Success criteria

- Each framework classified by mechanism type
- Assessment: do working systems correlate with attractors and failing systems with gates?
- If yes: the gate/attractor dimension is externally validated
- If no: the distinction is orch-go-specific — document what the external pattern actually is

## Key files

- .kb/investigations/2026-03-22-research-test-coordination-protocol-primitives-external-frameworks.md
- .kb/models/knowledge-accretion/probes/2026-03-20-probe-intervention-effectiveness-audit.md (the 31-intervention audit)
- .kb/models/knowledge-accretion/probes/2026-03-20-probe-governance-infrastructure-self-accretion.md

title: Rewrite README around thread/comprehension layer
description:
Context:
The project boundary has shifted. orch-go is now explicitly framed as the thread/comprehension/coordination layer, not primarily an orchestration CLI. README.md still introduces the project in orchestration/OpenCode terms, which reproduces the old identity for every new reader.

What's needed:
Rewrite the top-level README so a new reader can understand the project as a system that turns agent work into durable, legible understanding across changing models and clients. Execution/orchestration should remain present, but as substrate rather than the main story.

Constraints:
- Align with .kb/decisions/2026-03-26-thread-comprehension-layer-is-primary-product.md
- Align with .kb/plans/2026-03-26-thread-comprehension-consolidation.md Phase 1
- Keep the README concrete and legible to an outsider; do not assume the reader knows internal vocabulary
- Preserve enough operational detail that the repo is still usable

Deliverable:
Updated README that clearly states the new product thesis, boundary, and relationship to execution substrate.

title: Daemon ignores skill: label when type-based inference overrides — task with skill:architect routes to feature-impl
description:
When an issue has both a type (task) and a skill: label (skill:architect), the daemon routes based on type (task -> feature-impl) and ignores the skill: label. Observed twice in led-totem-toppers:

1. led-totem-toppers-svc: type=task, labels=[skill:architect], routed as feature-impl (light tier)
2. led-totem-toppers-jr5: type=task, labels=[skill:architect effort:large], routed as feature-impl (light tier)

Both times the skill:architect label was explicitly added to override type inference (per orchestrator enrichment protocol), but the daemon used the type fallback anyway. The enrichment protocol says skill: labels should lift routing from the 69% type-fallback — but the daemon appears to ignore them.

Expected: skill: label takes precedence over type-based inference.
Actual: type-based inference wins, skill: label is ignored.

title: Architect: design artifact lifecycle state model for kb reflect filtering
description:
ORIENTATION_FRAME: kb reflect keeps finding things it shouldn't — archived files, template placeholders, already-promoted investigations, already-existing guides. 4+ exclusion patches have been applied (archived dirs, synthesized dirs, template Next: fields, existing guides/decisions check). Each fix adds another exclusion rule. Classic Coherence Over Patches signal.

The missing coherent model: reflect has no concept of "artifact lifecycle state" (active / archived / promoted / template). It treats all files as live candidates, then patches keep teaching it to ignore specific non-live states one at a time.

INVESTIGATE:
1. Catalog all current exclusion/filter patches in kb reflect (grep for exclude, skip, filter, ignore patterns)
2. Identify the artifact lifecycle states that exist implicitly (active, archived, promoted, synthesized, template/placeholder)
3. Design a coherent lifecycle state model that replaces N exclusion patches with one state check
4. Consider: should state live in frontmatter? directory convention? both?
5. Assess whether the dedup problem (3 patches for --create-issue dedup) is the same missing model or separate

Prior patches (commits):
- 015e6d9 fix: exclude archived/synthesized dirs and use filename-based age
- 99261cf fix: exclude archived/ directories from kb reflect --type open
- d18eeb1 Fix kb reflect --type open to filter out template placeholder Next: fields
- e68ef91 fix: check for existing guides/decisions in kb reflect synthesis detection
- 8b3f1c8 feat: kb reflect synthesis detects model existence and excludes archived/synthesized
- 72831c2 fix: semantic clustering for kb reflect synthesis

Target repo: ~/Documents/personal/kb-cli
Principle: Coherence Over Patches — ~/.kb/decisions/2026-01-04-coherence-over-patches.md

title: [orch-go] architect: Dylan has 3 dashboard views that need architect...
Design knowledge graph visualization for dashboard: claim tension graph, cluster detection, health heatmap
description: Architect task: Design a Knowledge tab for the dashboard (localhost:5188, Svelte) that visualizes the claim graph. Three views: (1) Force-directed tension graph — claims as nodes colored by model, tension edges (confirms/extends/contradicts), hub clusters visually obvious. Click cluster to see member claims + last validated dates. (2) Claim health heatmap — per-model grid of confirmed/stale/unconfirmed claims. (3) Model relationship map — models as nodes, edges weighted by cross-model tension count. Data source: claims.yaml files (88+ claims, 8 models, 41+ tensions). title: [orch-go] architect: Design a hook testing and observability system ...
Fix orch clean gap: call IsSessionProcessing for ALL untracked sessions
description: cleanUntrackedDiskSessions() in clean_cmd.go:464-485 has a 5-minute recency threshold. Sessions idle >5min WITHOUT a .session_id workspace file bypass the IsSessionProcessing() check entirely and get DELETED. Interactive/TUI orchestrator sessions have no workspace, so they have zero Layer 1 protection. Fix: call IsSessionProcessing() for ALL untracked sessions, not just recently active ones. Cost: one API call per untracked session. See model: .kb/models/session-deletion-vectors.md (Vector #2)

title: Investigate Claude Code background process wakeup — can external events awaken an orchestrator session?
description:
## Question
Claude Code has a background execution feature where it can run commands asynchronously and get notified when they complete (the agent 'wakes up' and processes the result). Dylan wants to understand: how does this work mechanically, is it configurable, and can external processes (daemon, tmux agents, completions) trigger the same wakeup?

## Why this matters
The orchestrator currently only processes completions when Dylan is in a conversation. If the daemon or a completing agent could wake up an idle orchestrator session, the orchestrator could process completions asynchronously — drain the comprehension queue without waiting for Dylan to start a session. This connects to the comprehension artifacts thread.

## Investigation angles
1. How does Claude Code's background process notification work? What mechanism delivers the result back to the conversation?
2. Is the wakeup mechanism accessible from outside Claude Code? (stdin injection, IPC, file watch, etc.)
3. Can a tmux-based Claude Code session receive external wakeup signals?
4. What are the security/stability implications of external processes injecting into a Claude Code conversation?
5. Are there existing Claude Code hooks or MCP patterns that already support event-driven wakeup?

## Constraint
This is exploration — we don't know what's possible yet. The investigation should map the mechanism before proposing integration designs.
